name: Yayoi Kusama
artStatement: My art originates from hallucinations only I can see. I translate the obsessive visions and the infinite nets of dots that have flooded my mind since childhood into paintings, sculptures, and environments. Through repetition and accumulation I seek the self-obliteration of the individual into the infinite — a love for the universe that erases the boundary between body and cosmos.

biography.heading: Early life in Japan
biography.text:
Yayoi Kusama was born in 1929 in Matsumoto, Japan, into a conservative, well-off family that ran a plant nursery. From childhood she experienced vivid hallucinations — fields of dots, nets and flashing lights, and the sensation of being engulfed by patterns — which she began to channel into obsessive drawing. These experiences, and a difficult relationship with her family, would become the wellspring of her art.

She trained briefly in the traditional Nihonga style of painting in Kyoto but found it stifling. Determined to escape the constraints of post-war Japan and its expectations for women, she corresponded with the American painter Georgia O'Keeffe for encouragement and, in the late 1950s, made her way to the United States.

biography.heading: New York and the avant-garde
biography.text:
Arriving in New York around 1958, Kusama threw herself into the avant-garde. She produced her Infinity Net paintings — vast canvases covered edge to edge with small, repetitive looping marks, a hypnotic, all-over field with no centre and no boundary. She then expanded into sculpture, covering furniture and everyday objects with proliferating soft, stuffed protrusions in her Accumulation works.

Through the 1960s she became a prominent and provocative figure, staging happenings, polka-dot body-painting events and anti-war performances, and creating her first mirrored Infinity Rooms. Working alongside and ahead of figures associated with Pop and Minimalism, she pursued ideas of repetition, obsession, infinity and the dissolution of the self — though as a Japanese woman she often did not receive due recognition at the time.

biography.heading: Return to Japan and the dots
biography.text:
In the early 1970s Kusama returned to Japan in poor health. Since 1977 she has, by her own choice, lived in a psychiatric hospital in Tokyo, working every day in a studio nearby. Far from ending her career, this period saw the steady deepening of her art and, eventually, her rise to global superstardom.

Her vocabulary — polka dots, nets, pumpkins, flowers, tentacular forms and mirrored infinity — became ever more concentrated. The dot, for Kusama, is both a symbol of the self and a means of "self-obliteration": by covering everything, including her own body, in dots, the individual dissolves into the infinite cosmos. What began as a way of coping with hallucination became a profound, universal vision of connection and endlessness.

biography.heading: Infinity Mirror Rooms
biography.text:
Kusama's Infinity Mirror Rooms are among the most beloved artworks of the contemporary era. Mirror-lined chambers filled with hanging lights, dotted forms or floating lanterns create the illusion of boundless, glittering space extending in every direction. Stepping inside, often alone and only for a minute, the viewer is enveloped in apparent infinity — an experience at once dazzling, intimate and slightly vertiginous.

These rooms distil her lifelong themes into immediate, overwhelming sensation. They have drawn record crowds at museums around the world and made her, improbably, one of the most popular artists alive, beloved far beyond the usual art audience.

biography.heading: Legacy
biography.text:
Now in her nineties and still working daily, Kusama is among the most successful and recognisable artists in the world. She has represented Japan at the Venice Biennale, been the subject of blockbuster international retrospectives, collaborated widely across fashion and design, and seen her pumpkins and dots become global icons.

Her achievement is monumental: from profound personal struggle she forged a singular visual language and a vision of infinity, repetition and the dissolution of the self that speaks to vast audiences. A pioneer too often overlooked in her youth, Kusama has lived to see herself recognised as one of the defining artists of her age.

name: El Anatsui
artStatement: I work with media that have a history of human touch — discarded bottle caps, printing plates, milk-tin lids. By stitching thousands of fragments together with copper wire I make works that are deliberately non-fixed: they can be draped, folded, and re-formed at every installation. Form should remain free. My materials carry the memory of trade, consumption, and the meeting of cultures across the Atlantic.

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
El Anatsui was born in 1944 in Anyako, in what is now Ghana, and trained at the College of Art at the University of Science and Technology in Kumasi. In 1975 he joined the University of Nigeria in Nsukka, becoming a central figure in the influential Nsukka school and spending much of his career in Nigeria. He is widely regarded as one of the most important African artists of his era and a major figure in global contemporary art.

Anatsui's work is rooted in West African traditions, materials and histories, yet speaks in a universal sculptural language. Across a long career he has worked with wood, clay and ceramics, but he is most celebrated for the monumental metal hangings he began making around the turn of the millennium.

biography.heading: The bottle-cap tapestries
biography.text:
Anatsui's most famous works are vast, shimmering "cloths" made from thousands of discarded aluminium bottle caps and seals from liquor bottles, flattened, folded and stitched together with copper wire. These enormous hangings drape and fold like textiles, catching the light with a metallic, golden brilliance that recalls royal kente cloth and other West African textile traditions.

Despite their grandeur, the works are made from waste — the detritus of consumption and trade. Each is flexible and has no fixed form; it is reshaped every time it is installed, so that the artwork is partly authored by those who hang it. This combination of monumental beauty, humble material and improvisatory installation is at the heart of Anatsui's achievement.

biography.heading: History, trade and transformation
biography.text:
Anatsui's materials carry deep historical resonance. The liquor-bottle caps evoke the transatlantic trade in which alcohol was exchanged for enslaved Africans and other goods, linking the works to the histories of colonialism, commerce and the African diaspora. By transforming this charged refuse into objects of splendour, Anatsui performs a kind of redemptive alchemy.

His art is thus both formally dazzling and conceptually rich, addressing consumption, waste, globalisation and the legacies of colonial exchange. The flexibility and reconfigurability of the hangings also speak to ideas of fluidity, adaptation and the provisional nature of form and meaning.

biography.heading: Process and collaboration
biography.text:
Anatsui's metal works are made collaboratively, with teams of assistants in his studio flattening caps and assembling sections that are then combined into the final composition. This workshop method connects his practice to communal traditions of making and to the social fabric of his Nigerian base.

Earlier in his career he was known for sculptures in wood — including works incised with motifs drawn from Adinkra symbols and broken or chainsawed forms evoking rupture and history — and for ceramics. These bodies of work share his interest in transformation, in the marks of process, and in materials charged with cultural meaning.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Anatsui has exhibited at major museums and biennials around the world, won the Golden Lion for Lifetime Achievement at the Venice Biennale, and created spectacular commissions including a monumental installation for the Turbine Hall at Tate Modern. His work is held in leading collections internationally.

His enduring contribution is to have created a wholly original sculptural language from the discarded materials of global trade — monumental, beautiful, flexible works that bridge African textile traditions and contemporary art while confronting histories of colonialism and consumption. Anatsui transformed waste into splendour and helped reshape the place of African art on the world stage.

name: Julie Mehretu
artStatement: My paintings are palimpsests — layered maps of architecture, migration, protest, and weather. I build dense fields of marks, gestures, and erased photographic source images so that history accumulates and dissolves at once. I am interested in the social agency of abstraction: how a non-figurative surface can hold the pressure of contemporary events without illustrating them.

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Julie Mehretu was born in 1970 in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. Her family left the country amid political turmoil when she was a child, settling in the United States. She studied at Kalamazoo College and earned her graduate degree at the Rhode Island School of Design, and is based in New York. She has become one of the most acclaimed abstract painters of her generation, known for monumental, densely layered canvases.

Mehretu's experience of migration and her engagement with history, politics and geography are fundamental to her work. Her paintings are abstract, but they are built from the imagery of maps, architecture, urban plans and historical events, fusing personal and global histories into vast, dynamic compositions.

biography.heading: Architectures of history
biography.text:
Mehretu's mature paintings layer fragments of architectural drawings, city maps, building plans, charts and diagrams beneath and within fields of gestural marks, lines and abstract forms. These references — to airports, stadiums, financial centres, sites of conflict and migration — ground the abstraction in the real structures and histories of the modern world.

Over these architectural substrata she builds dynamic networks of marks: sweeping lines, dots, dashes and explosive bursts that suggest movement, energy, crowds, weather systems or social upheaval. The result is a sense of history in motion — of populations, capital, power and conflict surging across and through the built environment. Her paintings function as palimpsests, layering time, place and event.

biography.heading: Abstraction and politics
biography.text:
Mehretu's work engages directly with global politics, migration, revolution and social change, while remaining resolutely abstract. Some paintings respond to specific events — uprisings, wars, the movements of refugees — by drawing on news imagery that she abstracts, blurs and layers, so that the political content is felt as force and structure rather than depicted literally.

This is abstraction as a way of grappling with the complexity of contemporary global reality — too vast and entangled to be captured in a single image, but registered in the dense, layered, energetic accumulation of marks. Mehretu has spoken of abstraction's freedom and its capacity to hold what cannot easily be named.

biography.heading: Scale and process
biography.text:
Mehretu often works on an enormous scale, with canvases spanning many metres, built up over long periods with the help of assistants. Her process involves layering — drawing, painting, printing, sanding and overpainting — so that the finished works contain deep histories of accumulation and erasure, much like the cities and histories they evoke.

She has created major site-specific commissions, including monumental paintings for public and corporate spaces, and her recent work has incorporated blurred, photographic grounds derived from images of contemporary events. Across all of it, the dialogue between underlying structure and overlaid gesture remains central.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Mehretu received a MacArthur Fellowship and has been the subject of major retrospectives, including a celebrated mid-career survey organised by the Whitney Museum and the Los Angeles County Museum of Art. Her work is held in leading collections worldwide and commands wide critical acclaim.

Her enduring contribution is to have created a powerful new form of history painting in the language of abstraction — vast, layered canvases that register the movements, structures and upheavals of the global present. Mehretu has expanded the ambitions of abstract painting, binding it to the urgent realities of migration, politics and the built world.

name: Anish Kapoor
artStatement: Kapoor's sculpture works between the monumental and the void — polished mirror-surfaces that dissolve the object into its surroundings, deep saturated pigment, and engineered voids that pull the eye into apparent bottomlessness. He is preoccupied with the negative, the immaterial and the sublime: the point at which a physical object becomes a perceptual and almost spiritual event.

headshots.credit: Anish Kapoor, 2017 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: Knighthood for services to the visual arts (2013)

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Public sculpture, mirror works and pigment pieces.

videos.title: Anish Kapoor at the Royal Academy of Arts
Anish Kapoor & Tim Marlow in Conversation

biography.heading: Early life and training
biography.text:
Anish Kapoor was born in Bombay (now Mumbai) in 1954 to a Punjabi Hindu father, who served as a hydrographer in the Indian Navy, and an Iraqi-Jewish mother. That double inheritance — and the experience of growing up between cultures and religions — left a lasting imprint on an artist whose work is repeatedly drawn to thresholds, dualities and states of in-betweenness. As a teenager he spent time on a kibbutz in Israel, briefly considering a career in engineering before deciding that his future lay in art.

In the early 1970s Kapoor moved to London, the city that has remained his base ever since. He studied at Hornsey College of Art and then at the Chelsea School of Art, emerging into a British art world that was beginning to rethink sculpture after the dominance of minimalism and conceptualism. A formative return trip to India in 1979 proved decisive: the powdered pigments piled in cones outside Hindu temples and markets suggested a way of making sculpture that was at once intensely material and almost immaterial — colour as substance, form as a kind of apparition.

biography.heading: Breakthrough: pigment and the void
biography.text:
Kapoor first drew serious attention in the early 1980s with works from the series later known as 1000 Names. These were modest geometric forms — mounds, cones, fragments — coated in raw, unmixed pigment in vivid reds, yellows and blues. The loose powder spilled onto the floor around each piece, so that the sculpture appeared to bleed into its surroundings and seemed to have no fixed edge. The colour was so saturated that it read less as a surface than as a glowing field, dissolving the object's mass.

He was associated with the loosely grouped "New British Sculpture" of that decade, alongside figures such as Tony Cragg and Richard Deacon, but his sensibility was distinct: more metaphysical, more concerned with absence than with assembled material. Through the later 1980s and 1990s he turned increasingly to the void — carving concavities into stone and wall, lining them with dark pigment so that the eye could not fix the depth. Standing before these works, viewers often cannot tell whether they are looking at a shallow dish or an infinite well. This deliberate destabilising of perception became one of the defining strategies of his career.

biography.heading: The mirror works and the public monument
biography.text:
From the late 1990s Kapoor began the body of work for which he is now most widely known: highly polished stainless-steel mirrors, concave and convex, that capture, invert and smear the world around them. These pieces turn the viewer into a participant — you cannot look at the work without seeing yourself, distorted, embedded in the sky and the crowd. The most celebrated is Cloud Gate (2004–2006) in Chicago's Millennium Park, the vast bean-shaped mirror affectionately nicknamed "The Bean," which has become one of the most photographed public artworks in the world.

Other landmark commissions followed: the towering Marsyas (2002) that filled the entire Turbine Hall at Tate Modern with a blood-red PVC membrane stretched between three steel rings; Sky Mirror, installed at sites from the Rockefeller Center to Kensington Gardens; and the ArcelorMittal Orbit (2012), the contorted observation tower built with the engineer Cecil Balmond for the London Olympic Park. These projects established Kapoor as one of the leading makers of large-scale public sculpture of his generation.

biography.heading: Materials, scale and the limits of colour
biography.text:
Kapoor's practice is unusually engaged with technology and material science. His "void" works depend on precisely engineered geometries; his mirrors require extraordinary fabrication tolerances. In the 2010s he became associated with Vantablack, one of the darkest substances ever made, which absorbs almost all visible light — a material that quite literally turns an object into a hole in space. His exclusive artistic licence to use it provoked a long-running and public feud with other artists, a controversy that itself spoke to his lifelong fascination with the absolute: pure colour, pure dark, pure reflection.

Equally provocative are his red wax and silicone works, in which mechanised arms smear and extrude great masses of blood-coloured material, and pieces that evoke the body and its wounds. Across these very different materials runs a consistent ambition — to make objects that behave like events, that seem to generate or swallow space rather than simply occupy it.

biography.heading: Reception, ideas and legacy
biography.text:
Kapoor won the Premio Duemila at the Venice Biennale in 1990 and the Turner Prize in 1991, and was later knighted for services to the visual arts. His exhibitions at the Royal Academy, the Grand Palais (the Monumenta commission Leviathan, 2011) and museums worldwide draw enormous audiences. Critics have sometimes questioned the spectacle and scale of the later work, but the underlying concerns have remained remarkably consistent since the 1980s.

At heart, Kapoor is an artist of the sublime and the negative — of the moment when a physical object becomes a perceptual and almost spiritual experience. His sculptures stage encounters with bottomlessness, reflection and saturated colour that ask the viewer to question the reliability of their own senses. Whether through a temple-bright pigment piece small enough to hold in the mind or a mirrored monument that contains a whole city, his work returns again and again to the same threshold: the point where matter seems to open onto something immaterial.

name: Olafur Eliasson
artStatement: Eliasson builds installations from elemental materials — light, water, fog, temperature and reflection — that make perception itself the subject of the work. By staging atmospheric phenomena indoors, he turns viewers into active participants who become aware of their own act of seeing, and increasingly extends that attention to climate and ecology.

headshots.credit: Olafur Eliasson, Berlinale 2017 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

education: Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts, Copenhagen (1989–1995)

videos.title: The Weather Project at Tate Modern
Olafur Eliasson: A Riverbed Inside the Museum

biography.heading: Background and formation
biography.text:
Olafur Eliasson was born in Copenhagen in 1967 to Icelandic parents, and spent much of his childhood between Denmark and Iceland. The Icelandic landscape — its glaciers, lava fields, waterfalls, moss and shifting northern light — became a lifelong reference point, not as scenery to be depicted but as a set of natural phenomena to be studied and reconstructed. Before art school he was, briefly, a breakdancer, and the bodily, performative awareness of that period carried into a practice always concerned with how people physically move through and respond to space.

He studied at the Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts from 1989 to 1995 and settled in Berlin, where he founded Studio Olafur Eliasson, today a large interdisciplinary workshop employing craftspeople, architects, engineers, cooks and researchers. From the start, Eliasson approached art less as the production of objects than as the construction of situations — experiments in perception in which the viewer completes the work.

biography.heading: Phenomena as medium
biography.text:
Eliasson's materials are light, water, mist, temperature, colour and movement. An early signature work, Beauty (1993), is simply a fine curtain of mist lit by a spotlight, in which a rainbow appears only when the viewer stands at the right angle — so that the artwork exists, strictly speaking, in the relationship between light, water and the individual eye. This insight — that perception is not passive reception but active construction — underlies almost everything he has made since.

He is fascinated by the apparatus behind an illusion and refuses to hide it. Fans, scaffolding, lamps, hoses and projectors are left visible, so that the viewer simultaneously experiences a sublime effect and understands exactly how it is produced. This double awareness — wonder and demystification at once — is central to his ethics: he wants people to feel that reality is something made, and therefore something that can be changed.

biography.heading: The Weather Project and global recognition
biography.text:
In 2003 Eliasson transformed the Turbine Hall of Tate Modern with The Weather Project, the Unilever Series commission that made him internationally famous. A giant semicircular "sun" of monofrequency lamps, doubled into a full circle by a mirrored ceiling, filled the vast hall with amber light and a fine haze. More than two million visitors came; many lay on the floor, gazing up at their tiny reflections in the mirror overhead, turning the museum into a strange communal space somewhere between a beach, a sunset and a public square.

The work crystallised his method: take a shared natural phenomenon — here, weather and the sun — and stage it indoors so that strangers experience it together and become aware of themselves perceiving. The social dimension, the sense of a temporary collective, has remained as important to him as the optical spectacle.

biography.heading: Environment, architecture and public life
biography.text:
Eliasson's interests increasingly converged on climate and the public realm. In 2008 he installed the New York City Waterfalls, four large artificial cascades in the East River. In 2014, with the geologist Minik Rosing, he brought blocks of glacial ice from a Greenland fjord to public squares in Copenhagen, Paris and London for Ice Watch, letting passers-by touch and watch the ice melt as a direct, tactile confrontation with global warming.

He has worked extensively with architecture and design — most notably the faceted glass façade of the Harpa concert hall in Reykjavík, developed with the architectural firm Henning Larsen. His socially oriented projects include Little Sun, a solar-powered lamp designed with the engineer Frederik Ottesen to bring clean light to communities without reliable electricity, and Green Light, a workshop project made with refugees and migrants. For Eliasson, the studio is a laboratory not only for art but for ways of living.

biography.heading: Ideas, reception and influence
biography.text:
Eliasson's work sits at the intersection of art, science, architecture and activism, and he has been a key figure in establishing the immersive, experiential installation as a central mode of contemporary art. Major retrospectives — including In real life at Tate Modern in 2019 — have drawn vast audiences, and his colour-experiment works, glass polyhedra, and "Your" series of perceptual pieces are widely collected.

Critics sometimes ask whether the popularity and spectacle of his installations risk reducing them to backdrops for photographs. Eliasson's answer is built into the work: by always exposing the machinery and emphasising the viewer's active role, he tries to convert spectacle into reflection. His enduring contribution is a body of work that treats perception itself as a civic matter — arguing that learning to see how we see is the first step toward taking responsibility for the world we share.

name: Jesús Rafael Soto
artStatement: A founder of kinetic and op art, Soto made works in which line, grid and suspended elements vibrate as the spectator moves, dissolving the boundary between object and space. His immersive 'Penetrables' invited viewers to walk into fields of hanging tubes, replacing the static picture with pure relational movement.

headshots.credit: Jesús Rafael Soto, photo by Lothar Wolleh — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: National Prize for Plastic Arts, Venezuela (1960)

biography.heading: Origins in Venezuela
biography.text:
Jesús Rafael Soto was born in 1923 in Ciudad Bolívar, on the banks of the Orinoco in Venezuela. He grew up in modest circumstances and earned money as a young man painting cinema posters and playing guitar, developing both a practical command of image-making and a musician's feeling for rhythm and structure that would later inform his abstract art. A scholarship took him to the School of Plastic Arts in Caracas, where he absorbed the fundamentals of painting before becoming impatient with its static conventions.

In 1950 Soto moved to Paris, the city that would be his home for the rest of his life. There he entered an extraordinary milieu of avant-garde abstraction and met artists who were rethinking the relationship between art, movement and the spectator — among them Victor Vasarely, and the circle that would soon be identified with kinetic art.

biography.heading: Vibration and the birth of kinetic art
biography.text:
Soto's central discovery was the idea of visual vibration. Working with grids, lines and superimposed transparent planes — often Plexiglas screens hung in front of striped backgrounds — he created surfaces that seem to shimmer, dissolve and pulse as the viewer moves past them. Nothing in the work mechanically moves; the movement is generated entirely in perception, by the relationship between the two layers and the shifting position of the eye.

He took part in the landmark 1955 exhibition Le Mouvement at the Galerie Denise René in Paris, the show that effectively launched kinetic art as an international tendency. For Soto, the goal was not optical trickery for its own sake but a profound shift in what an artwork is: not a fixed object to be contemplated from a distance, but a dynamic field activated by time and the moving body of the viewer.

biography.heading: The Penetrables
biography.text:
From the 1960s Soto developed the body of work that most fully realised his ambitions: the Penetrables. These are immersive environments made of thousands of thin, dangling nylon or plastic tubes hung from a frame, forming a soft rectangular volume that visitors are invited to walk into. Inside, the rigid distinction between viewer and artwork collapses entirely. The body is enveloped; the surrounding lines blur and vibrate; one becomes, quite literally, part of the work.

The Penetrables transformed Soto's investigation of vibration from something seen into something inhabited. His final and most ambitious example, the shimmering golden Houston Penetrable created for the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston and completed after his death, allowed visitors to disappear into a glowing curtain of hand-painted strands. These works remain among the most beloved participatory artworks of the twentieth century.

biography.heading: Public art and the dematerialised object
biography.text:
Soto believed that art should leave the museum and become part of everyday civic life. He produced a vast body of public commissions — murals, suspended volumes and architectural integrations — across Venezuela and the world, including large works for public buildings and squares. His suspended "virtual volumes," in which painted rods hang in space to suggest a cube or sphere that has no solid existence, epitomise his lifelong aim: to dematerialise the art object and replace solid form with pure relationship and energy.

In 1973 he founded the Museo de Arte Moderno Jesús Soto in his home city of Ciudad Bolívar, a museum dedicated to kinetic and constructivist art that remains a major cultural institution in Venezuela and a testament to his commitment to bringing advanced abstraction to a broad public.

biography.heading: Legacy
biography.text:
Soto died in Paris in 2005, recognised as one of the founding figures of kinetic and Op-related art and the most internationally celebrated Venezuelan artist of his century, alongside his compatriot Carlos Cruz-Diez. His work is held in major museums worldwide and has been the subject of important retrospectives.

His enduring achievement was to reconceive the artwork as an event in time rather than a thing in space. By making vibration, immersion and the spectator's movement the true content of his art, Soto helped open the path toward installation and participatory art, and demonstrated that abstraction could be not cold and distant but bodily, joyful and alive.

name: Carlos Cruz-Diez
artStatement: Cruz-Diez treated colour as an autonomous, time-based event rather than a property of form. Across his Physichromie, Chromosaturation and Chromointerference series he engineered situations in which colour is generated in the eye of the moving viewer, making perception unstable, additive and continuously in flux.

headshots.credit: Carlos Cruz-Diez, France 2013 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Series investigating colour as a perceptual event.

biography.heading: Formation in Caracas
biography.text:
Carlos Cruz-Diez was born in Caracas, Venezuela, in 1923. He trained at the School of Fine Arts in Caracas and worked for years as an illustrator, graphic designer and art director — including a period running a design studio and teaching — before fully committing to the radical investigation of colour that would define his life. This long apprenticeship in applied image-making gave him an unusually rigorous, almost scientific approach to his medium.

Like his contemporary Jesús Rafael Soto, Cruz-Diez gravitated toward Europe, dividing his life between Caracas and Paris, where he settled in 1960. In the ferment of post-war Parisian abstraction he found kindred spirits in the kinetic movement, but he carved out a distinctive territory entirely his own: colour not as a fixed property of objects, but as an autonomous, unstable, time-based reality.

biography.heading: Colour as event
biography.text:
Cruz-Diez's foundational idea was that colour is not something a surface has, but something that happens — an event that unfolds in the encounter between light, surface, time and the moving viewer. To explore this he developed several long-running families of work, each a kind of experimental apparatus.

In the Physichromie series, begun in 1959, narrow coloured strips are mounted on a relief of thin vertical fins, so that as the viewer walks past, colours mix optically and shift continuously — the work seems to radiate a coloured light that has no stable existence. The Couleur Aditiva and Induction Chromatique works exploit afterimages and the way adjacent colours alter one another, demonstrating that what we "see" is constructed by the eye and brain rather than simply received.

biography.heading: The Chromosaturation
biography.text:
Cruz-Diez's most immersive invention is the Chromosaturation, first conceived around 1965: a set of adjoining rooms or chambers each flooded with a single intense colour of light — typically red, green and blue. As visitors pass from one chamber to the next, their perception is so saturated by one hue that the others appear extraordinarily vivid, and the very air seems to become a tangible, coloured substance.

The Chromosaturation removes colour entirely from objects and places it in space itself, surrounding the body and disorienting the senses. It is a pure demonstration of his thesis: that colour is an evolving situation, a physical experience in real time, rather than a decorative attribute. These environments have been recreated in museums around the world and remain among the most popular participatory works of post-war art.

biography.heading: Public art and national identity
biography.text:
Cruz-Diez was deeply committed to integrating art into everyday public space. His most famous work in this vein is the vast chromatic floor of the Simón Bolívar International Airport in Maiquetía, Venezuela — a sweeping pattern of coloured bands that greets every traveller arriving in the country and has become an unofficial national emblem. He produced numerous other architectural integrations, pavements, murals and even a hydroelectric dam project, insisting that his colour research belonged to the public realm as much as to the gallery.

For Cruz-Diez, this public dimension was inseparable from a democratic ideal: art that does not require specialist knowledge, that anyone can experience directly through their own perception, and that turns the routine spaces of modern life into sites of wonder.

biography.heading: Method, recognition and legacy
biography.text:
Cruz-Diez approached his work with the discipline of a researcher, defining precise categories of chromatic phenomena and pursuing each through decades of methodical experiment. He established a studio and archive in Paris and a foundation dedicated to preserving and studying his work, and his pieces entered the collections of major museums including the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston, which holds a significant body of his work, and the Tate.

He died in Paris in 2019. Together with Soto, he is regarded as a giant of kinetic art and the leading theorist-practitioner of colour of his generation. His legacy is a body of work that permanently changed how artists think about colour — proving that it is not a static given but a living, perceptual, participatory event.

name: James Turrell
artStatement: A central figure of the Light and Space movement, Turrell treats light as a physical, almost tangible material. His Skyspaces, Ganzfelds and the decades-long Roden Crater frame and shape light and sky so that perception turns inward — 'I make spaces that apprehend light for our perception,' he has said, 'and in some ways gather it, or seem to hold it.'

headshots.credit: James Turrell — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Light works, Skyspaces and the Roden Crater project.

videos.title: Aten Reign at the Guggenheim & the Skyspaces
Exploring Roden Crater & Quaker Meeting House
James Turrell's Roden Crater: The South Space

biography.heading: A Quaker upbringing and the science of light
biography.text:
James Turrell was born in Los Angeles in 1943 into a Quaker family. The Quaker emphasis on inner light, silence and direct, unmediated experience of the divine runs like a thread through his entire body of work, which treats light not as a tool for illuminating objects but as a physical, almost spiritual substance in its own right. As a young man he obtained a pilot's licence, and the experience of flight — of sky, atmosphere and the curvature of space — became another foundational influence.

He studied perceptual psychology at Pomona College and later art at the University of California, Irvine, and at Claremont. In the mid-1960s he became part of the loose grouping of Southern Californian artists associated with the "Light and Space" movement, who shared an interest in perception, atmosphere and the subtle conditions of seeing rather than in solid, autonomous objects.

biography.heading: The Mendota Stoppage and projection works
biography.text:
Turrell's early breakthrough came in his studio in a former hotel in Santa Monica, the Mendota, where he sealed the windows and controlled the admission of light to study how the eye behaves in carefully manipulated conditions. From these experiments came his Projection Pieces, in which a single high-intensity projector throws light into the corner of a darkened room so precisely that it appears to be a solid, glowing three-dimensional form hovering in space.

These works established the central paradox of his art: there is, materially, almost nothing there — only light and a room — yet the viewer perceives something with apparent mass and presence. Turrell's subject is not the light itself so much as the act of perception, the way the brain constructs a world from what enters the eye. He often says that his medium is not light but "your seeing."

biography.heading: Skyspaces and Ganzfeld
biography.text:
Among Turrell's most widely experienced works are the Skyspaces: enclosed chambers, dozens of which now exist around the world, with a precisely cut aperture open to the sky. Sitting inside, especially at dawn or dusk while concealed LED lighting subtly shifts the colour of the surrounding walls, the viewer sees the sky transformed — it appears to flatten into a vivid, almost solid plane of pure colour stretched across the opening. The familiar sky becomes strange and intensely present.

His Ganzfeld works immerse the viewer in a uniform, edgeless field of coloured light so complete that depth perception fails entirely and one loses any sense of distance or surface. These pieces, like much of his work, induce a meditative, slightly vertiginous state — an encounter with perception at the very edge of its capacities.

biography.heading: Roden Crater
biography.text:
Since 1977 Turrell has devoted much of his life to a single monumental project: Roden Crater, an extinct volcanic cinder cone in the Painted Desert of northern Arizona that he is transforming into a vast naked-eye observatory. Tunnels, chambers and apertures are being engineered to frame celestial events — the movements of the sun, moon and stars, and phenomena such as the 18.6-year lunar cycle — turning the entire crater into an instrument for experiencing light and cosmic time.

Decades in the making and still unfinished, Roden Crater is one of the most ambitious works of land art ever attempted, a successor to the great earthworks of the 1970s but tuned entirely to perception and light. It represents the fullest expression of Turrell's lifelong aim: to create spaces in which people can, in his words, experience light directly and "see themselves seeing."

biography.heading: Reception and significance
biography.text:
Turrell has received many of the highest honours in the field, including a MacArthur Fellowship and the National Medal of Arts, and his retrospectives — including a celebrated 2013 installation that filled the rotunda of the Guggenheim in New York with shifting coloured light — have been among the most popular museum events of recent decades.

His influence is immense: he is the central figure in establishing light as a primary artistic medium, and a touchstone for generations of artists working with perception, immersion and atmosphere. Yet his work resists spectacle. At its core it is contemplative and quiet, asking only that the viewer slow down, attend to the act of seeing, and rediscover light as the most extraordinary material of all.

name: Ann Veronica Janssens
artStatement: Janssens works with light, colour, mist and reflection to produce ephemeral, destabilising experiences. Her fog-filled rooms saturate space with coloured artificial haze until depth and edges dissolve, foregrounding the fragility and physicality of perception itself.

headshots.credit: Studio portrait

videos.title: Ann Veronica Janssens: Passion for Light
Ann Veronica Janssens: Creating an Optical Illusion

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Ann Veronica Janssens was born in 1956 in Folkestone, England, to Belgian parents, and has lived and worked in Brussels for most of her career. She emerged as an artist in the 1980s and developed a practice rooted in experimental, materially minimal but perceptually overwhelming installation. From the beginning she has been less interested in making permanent objects than in creating fleeting, almost weightless experiences that test the limits of vision and the senses.

Her chosen materials are among the least solid imaginable: artificial mist, projected and reflected light, transparent and coloured glass, sprays of colour, and the simple architecture of the spaces she works in. This commitment to the ephemeral aligns her with a broader European and international tendency toward immersive, perception-based art, but her work has a distinctive lightness, intimacy and economy of means.

biography.heading: Mist, light and disorientation
biography.text:
Janssens is best known for her mist environments, in which a gallery is filled with dense artificial fog, often coloured by saturated coloured light. Visitors entering these rooms find their normal spatial bearings dissolving: walls disappear, distance becomes unreadable, other people loom and fade, and the air itself seems to become a soft, luminous, tangible substance. The works — among them the much-travelled yellowbluepink — produce a state of gentle disorientation that is at once physical and contemplative.

These environments embody her central concern: to make the act of perception itself the content of the work. By removing the usual visual cues, she returns the viewer to a heightened, almost newborn attentiveness, in which seeing becomes an active, uncertain, exhilarating effort rather than a passive given.

biography.heading: Glass, colour and the slippery image
biography.text:
Alongside the mist works Janssens makes more compact sculptures and interventions that explore similar phenomena. She uses sheets and blocks of glass, sometimes treated with coloured or dichroic films that shift hue depending on the angle of view, projecting drifting fields of colour onto floors and walls. She has worked with sprayed pigment, with reflective and transparent surfaces, and with simple optical devices that fracture and multiply light.

Many of her works hover at the boundary between something and nothing — a slick of colour that may be a solid object or merely a reflection, a luminous haze that cannot be grasped. This deliberate instability, the refusal of the image to settle, invites a slow, curious, embodied looking that is characteristic of her whole project.

biography.heading: Light, time and the everyday sublime
biography.text:
Janssens often incorporates natural phenomena and the passage of time. She has used sunlight, mirrors and prismatic films to scatter spectra across architecture, and has made works that depend on the changing light of the day. There is a recurring sense in her art of catching the sublime in the most ordinary materials — fog, sunlight, a pane of glass — and of revealing that wonder is available in the simple conditions of perception, if only we attend to them.

Her installations are frequently described as joyful and even euphoric, but they also carry a quiet philosophical seriousness about the fragility and constructedness of experience. Nothing is fixed; everything shimmers, drifts and depends on where you stand and how you look.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Over four decades Janssens has exhibited widely across Europe and internationally, with major presentations at institutions including the Wellcome Collection in London, the Louisiana Museum in Denmark and numerous European museums and biennials, and she represented Belgium at the Venice Biennale. Her mist works in particular have become emblematic of the contemporary appetite for immersive, sensory art.

Her enduring contribution lies in showing how much can be achieved with how little — how a room of coloured fog or a sliver of dichroic glass can produce an experience of disorientation, beauty and self-awareness as profound as any monumental sculpture. She remains one of the most admired European artists working with light and perception.

name: Tomás Saraceno
artStatement: Trained as an architect, Saraceno makes floating sculptures, vast cable webs and air-fuelled balloons that imagine new, fossil-free ways of inhabiting the planet. Drawing on spider silk, thermodynamics and the atmosphere, his Aerocene and Cloud Cities projects propose a speculative, ecological reorganisation of how we live and move.

headshots.credit: Tomás Saraceno, Florence 2020 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Tomás Saraceno: The Art of Noticing

biography.heading: From architecture to art
biography.text:
Tomás Saraceno was born in San Miguel de Tucumán, Argentina, in 1973, and trained as an architect before turning to art. That architectural background is fundamental to his work, which is preoccupied with structure, lightness, suspension and the dream of new ways of inhabiting space. He later studied art in Buenos Aires and Frankfurt and participated in a residency at the International Space Studies Program — an indication of the scientific and even cosmic ambitions that animate his practice.

Saraceno's work is collaborative and research-driven to an unusual degree. His Berlin studio functions as a hub connecting artists with arachnologists, astrophysicists, engineers, climate scientists and balloonists. He approaches art as a form of speculative, interdisciplinary inquiry into how humanity might live more lightly and more ethically on — and above — the Earth.

biography.heading: Floating worlds and Cloud Cities
biography.text:
A central thread of Saraceno's art is the vision of floating, airborne habitats. In sprawling installations such as the Cloud Cities and In Orbit, he constructs vast networks of inflated transparent spheres and tensile steel webs, sometimes strong enough for visitors to climb into and crawl across, suspended high above the floor of a museum. These structures evoke clouds, foam, soap bubbles and cellular networks, and propose — half utopian, half poetic — the possibility of cities that drift in the atmosphere, freed from national borders and fossil-fuelled ground.

These works combine genuine engineering with science-fiction imagination. They ask the viewer to experience weightlessness and interconnection bodily, and to entertain a radically different relationship to gravity, territory and the air we all share.

biography.heading: Spiders, webs and the more-than-human
biography.text:
Saraceno is among the foremost artistic researchers of spider webs, which he studies, scans and exhibits as models of complex, three-dimensional architecture built with minimal material. He has developed techniques to map webs in three dimensions and to let multiple spiders of different species build hybrid structures, presenting the results as delicate, illuminated sculptures suspended in darkened rooms.

For Saraceno the spider and its web are more than a formal inspiration; they are a model for thinking beyond the human, for attending to other forms of intelligence and other ways of constructing and sensing the world. His work consistently argues for a decentring of the human and a recognition of the planet as a dense web of interdependent life.

biography.heading: Aerocene and a fossil-free future
biography.text:
Out of these concerns grew Aerocene, an open-source artistic and scientific community founded by Saraceno around the idea of flight without fuel. Aerocene sculptures are buoyant balloons lifted into the air solely by the heat of the sun and the infrared radiation of the Earth — no helium, no propane, no carbon emissions. In a landmark series of flights, including human flights in the Argentine salt flats of Salinas Grandes, these solar balloons demonstrated emissions-free aerial travel.

Aerocene is at once an artwork, an environmental movement and a research platform, embodying Saraceno's conviction that art can model concrete alternatives to a fossil-fuel economy. It connects his aesthetic fascination with floating and lightness to an urgent ecological politics.

biography.heading: Significance
biography.text:
Saraceno has exhibited at major institutions and biennials worldwide, including a celebrated installation on the roof of the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York and large solo presentations at the Palais de Tokyo in Paris and elsewhere. His work has been recognised for the way it fuses art, architecture, natural science and activism into a single visionary project.

His enduring importance lies in his expansion of what an artwork can be: not an object but an ecosystem, a research network, a proposal for living differently. By turning museums into floating webs and the sky into a fuel-free commons, Saraceno offers some of the most hopeful and imaginative responses in contemporary art to the planetary crisis.

name: Conrad Shawcross
artStatement: Shawcross builds mechanical and geometric sculptures rooted in scientific and philosophical ideas — light, motion, harmonics and the limits of knowledge. His kinetic machines and perforated structures translate abstract systems into physical objects that perform, cast shifting shadows, or reach toward the impossible.

headshots.credit: Conrad Shawcross, 2011 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Mechanical sculpture and architectural light works.

works.title: The Dappled Light of the Sun

videos.title: Timepiece in action at the Roundhouse
Turning Science into Art | Brilliant Ideas

biography.heading: Background and influences
biography.text:
Conrad Shawcross was born in London in 1977 into a literary family — his parents are both writers — and studied at the Ruskin School of Drawing and Fine Art in Oxford and then at the Slade School of Fine Art in London. He emerged in the early 2000s as one of the most ambitious sculptors of his British generation, distinguished by a deep engagement with science, mathematics, philosophy and engineering.

Shawcross builds machines and structures that grapple with large ideas: the nature of time, the limits of human knowledge, the geometry of space, and the relationship between order and chance. Yet his work is never merely illustrative of science. He is fascinated precisely by the points where rational systems reach their edge and tip into mystery, paradox or the sublime.

biography.heading: Machines, light and motion
biography.text:
Many of Shawcross's best-known works are kinetic — intricate mechanical sculptures that move with hypnotic precision. In his Timepiece installations and related works, robotic arms carry light sources that trace slow, complex orbits, casting shifting shadows that map the passage of time in ways that depart from the conventional clock. His ADA project pairs a dancing industrial robot with light and choreography, named after the nineteenth-century mathematician Ada Lovelace.

These works draw on the history of scientific instruments — orreries, harmonographs, looms — while pushing them toward poetry. The mechanical exactness of the motion contrasts with the elusive, even melancholy questions the works pose about time, knowledge and our place in a vast and indifferent cosmos.

biography.heading: Geometry and the Paradigm series
biography.text:
A recurring formal language in Shawcross's sculpture is the tetrahedron and related geometric units, which he stacks, rotates and multiplies into towering structures. The Paradigm series — including a major permanent sculpture for the Francis Crick Institute in London — consists of stacked tetrahedra that twist and expand as they rise, an emblem of growth, knowledge and instability.

His Optic and lattice works explore how simple geometric rules can generate enormous complexity, and how the eye and mind respond to pattern, repetition and structure. Throughout, Shawcross treats geometry not as cold abstraction but as a charged, almost spiritual order underlying the physical world.

biography.heading: Public commissions and scale
biography.text:
Shawcross has become one of Britain's leading makers of large public sculpture. His Timepiece filled the Roundhouse in London; his Three Perpetual Chords were installed in Dulwich Park; and his monumental work The Optic Cloak forms part of the energy-centre flue on the Greenwich Peninsula, a vast perforated tower that doubles as public art. He has also made major works for sites ranging from gardens to galleries internationally.

These commissions demonstrate his ability to translate intellectually demanding ideas into objects of genuine public presence and beauty — structures that reward both casual encounter and sustained contemplation, and that hold their own in architectural and landscape settings.

biography.heading: Standing and ideas
biography.text:
Shawcross was elected a Royal Academician, one of the youngest artists to receive the honour, and his work is held in significant public and private collections. He is widely regarded as a leading figure in the contemporary dialogue between art and science.

At the centre of his practice is a productive humility about knowledge. His machines and geometries model our attempts to measure, order and understand the universe, while continually revealing how much escapes those attempts. The result is a body of work that is at once rigorously engineered and deeply philosophical — sculpture as a tool for thinking about time, cognition and the limits of certainty.

name: Alicja Kwade
artStatement: Kwade's sculptures and installations probe the subjectivity of time, space and value, using mirrors, stone, metal and everyday objects to stage quiet perceptual paradoxes. Reflections and rotations multiply and displace objects until the viewer can no longer trust what is real, original or merely its double.

headshots.credit: Alicja Kwade, Berlin 2014, photo by Oliver Mark — Image via Wikimedia Commons

series.name: Selected Works
series.description: Installations on time, space and perception.

videos.title: Alicja Kwade: Time, Space and Gravity

biography.heading: From Poland to Berlin
biography.text:
Alicja Kwade was born in 1979 in Katowice, Poland, and moved to Germany as a child after her family emigrated in the late 1980s. She studied at the Berlin University of the Arts, and Berlin has remained the centre of her life and work. She came to international prominence in the 2010s as one of the most intellectually compelling sculptors of her generation, known for elegant, precise installations that probe the structures of reality.

Kwade's work draws on physics, philosophy and everyday experience to question the systems through which we measure and make sense of the world — time, money, space, matter — and to expose how arbitrary and constructed those systems often are. Her sculptures are visually spare and beautifully made, but they are essentially instruments for philosophical doubt.

biography.heading: Stone, mirror and the doubling of the world
biography.text:
Among Kwade's signature materials are stone spheres, polished metal, glass and, above all, the mirror. In major installations such as the much-exhibited works built around steel frames and two-way mirrors, she arranges boulders, everyday objects and their cast or carved doubles within grids of mirrored panels, so that the viewer can no longer tell which objects are "real," which are reflections and which are copies. Walking through these environments, one's certainty about what is solid and what is illusion steadily erodes.

This strategy of doubling and reflection runs throughout her work. By presenting an object alongside a near-identical version in a different material — a stone and its bronze twin, a real branch and its cast copy — she destabilises the link between appearance and substance, and turns simple looking into a vertiginous epistemological puzzle.

biography.heading: Time, measurement and value
biography.text:
Kwade is fascinated by the abstract systems humans impose on the world. She has made clocks that behave strangely, works built from the bent hands of timepieces, and pieces that treat time as a malleable, almost physical material rather than a fixed, universal flow. Other works engage with money and value — melting down or transforming currency and objects to reveal the conventions underlying what we treat as worth.

Underlying all of this is a consistent question: how much of what we take to be objective reality is in fact a set of agreed-upon fictions? Her sculptures make those fictions visible by gently breaking them — a clock that runs wrong, a stone that turns out to be its own reflection — and in doing so they open a space for wonder and unease.

biography.heading: Public installations and cosmic scale
biography.text:
Kwade has increasingly worked at architectural and public scale. Her installation on the roof of the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, ParaPivot, suspended great stone spheres within intersecting steel frames, evoking a planetary system or orrery and inviting visitors to imagine the cosmos as a precarious, beautiful structure. Similar large-scale works have appeared on the Place Vendôme in Paris and in sculpture parks and public spaces internationally.

At this scale her concerns take on a cosmological dimension: the stones become planets, the frames become the abstract coordinates by which we map space, and the viewer is positioned as a small observer within vast, humanly invented systems of order. The effect is both serene and quietly destabilising.

biography.heading: Reception
biography.text:
Kwade has exhibited at leading museums and biennials worldwide, including the Venice Biennale, and her work is held in major international collections. She is widely recognised as one of the most significant sculptors to emerge from Europe in the twenty-first century.

Her achievement is to have made a body of work that is at once formally refined and philosophically searching — sculpture that looks like clarity itself yet is designed to undermine our confidence in the real. By turning the mirror, the stone and the clock into tools of doubt, Kwade asks us to recognise how thoroughly our sense of the world is shaped by the systems we have built to describe it.

name: Tomás Sánchez
artStatement: Sánchez paints meticulous, meditative landscapes — vast skies, still waters, dense forests and a solitary figure absorbed in contemplation — informed by his long practice of meditation. A counter-series of monumental landfills (basureros) sets that serenity against images of human waste and ecological reckoning.

headshots.credit: Landscape painting by Tomás Sánchez — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: A Cuban beginning
biography.text:
Tomás Sánchez was born in 1948 in Aguada de Pasajeros, in the province of Cienfuegos, Cuba. He studied at the prestigious Escuela Nacional de Arte and the national art instructors' school in Havana, training in a period when Cuban art was strongly shaped by revolutionary politics and social themes. Sánchez, however, gravitated toward something more inward and contemplative: the landscape, and the spiritual states it could evoke.

A practitioner of meditation, Sánchez developed an art rooted in stillness and inner experience at a time when such concerns were unfashionable. This commitment to contemplation, rather than to overt political content, set him apart and would become the foundation of an international career. He later left Cuba, living and working in Costa Rica and Miami, but the lush tropical landscape of his homeland remained his enduring subject.

biography.heading: The meditative landscape
biography.text:
Sánchez is celebrated for hyper-detailed, luminous paintings of nature — vast skies, still lagoons, dense jungle, solitary shorelines — rendered with extraordinary technical precision. His landscapes are not topographical records of real places but composed, idealised visions: serene, silent, almost supernatural in their clarity. Light pours through towering cloud formations; water lies utterly calm; a lone meditating figure or a tiny path may be the only sign of human presence.

These works descend from the great traditions of Romantic and luminist landscape painting, but they are filtered through Sánchez's meditative practice. The paintings function as objects of contemplation, inviting the viewer into the same quiet, expansive state of mind from which they were made. They are, in effect, visual equivalents of meditation.

biography.heading: Paradise and its shadow
biography.text:
Crucial to Sánchez's art is a counterpoint to the paradisal landscape: his depictions of garbage dumps and wastelands. In stark contrast to the pristine lagoons, these paintings show sprawling fields of refuse, sometimes with a small human figure picking through the debris. Placed within his body of work, they function as a moral and ecological warning — a vision of what humanity is doing to the natural paradise he so lovingly depicts elsewhere.

This pairing of the sublime and the spoiled gives Sánchez's work an ethical depth beyond mere beauty. The serene landscapes become not escapist fantasies but images of something precious and threatened, and the dumps become a sobering reminder of human responsibility. Together they form a sustained meditation on nature, consciousness and care.

biography.heading: Technique and discipline
biography.text:
Sánchez's paintings are renowned for their meticulous craft. Working in oil and acrylic, he builds up landscapes of astonishing detail — every leaf, ripple and cloud rendered with patient exactitude — to achieve an almost photographic, yet heightened and dreamlike, realism. He is also an accomplished draughtsman, producing intricate ink drawings of jungle and foliage.

This discipline is inseparable from his contemplative practice; the slow, attentive labour of painting is itself a form of meditation. The results have made him one of the most technically admired and commercially successful Latin American painters of his generation, with works held in major collections and achieving record prices for a Cuban artist.

biography.heading: Standing and legacy
biography.text:
Sánchez has exhibited internationally for decades and is regarded as one of the foremost contemporary landscape painters of Latin America. He has collaborated with environmental causes, reflecting the ecological concern embedded in his art, and his paintings are sought after by museums and private collectors across the Americas and Europe.

His enduring contribution is to have kept alive, and renewed, the spiritual landscape tradition in an age of conceptual and political art. By fusing breathtaking technical skill with a genuine meditative practice and an ecological conscience, Sánchez created a body of work that offers both serene beauty and a quiet, urgent reminder of what stands to be lost.

name: Bridget Riley
artStatement: A central figure of Op art, Riley makes precise abstract paintings of lines, curves and colour that set the eye in motion — producing shimmer, after-images and a sense of instability that locates the artwork in the act of looking itself.

headshots.credit: Portrait

videos.title: Bridget Riley’s Vision: Colour, Line and Perception

biography.heading: Early life and training
biography.text:
Bridget Riley was born in London in 1931. Her childhood was shaped by the Second World War, much of it spent in Cornwall, where the changing light, sea and landscape gave her an early, intense education in looking. She studied at Goldsmiths College and then at the Royal College of Art in London in the early 1950s, alongside a generation of artists who would shape post-war British art.

Riley's formative years were marked by careful study of the past. She copied a painting by the pointillist Georges Seurat, an exercise that taught her how separate marks of colour interact in the eye, and she absorbed lessons from old masters and from the Futurists. This grounding in the science and history of perception, rather than spontaneous self-expression, would define her entire career.

biography.heading: The black-and-white breakthrough
biography.text:
In the early 1960s Riley arrived at the work that made her famous: precise, hard-edged black-and-white paintings built from repeated geometric units — ovals, triangles, curves and lines — arranged so that the static surface appears to shimmer, swell, tilt and move. Paintings such as Movement in Squares and Fall seem to vibrate and destabilise before the eye, producing sensations of motion, dazzle and even dizziness.

These works placed her at the centre of what came to be called Op art. The 1965 exhibition The Responsive Eye at the Museum of Modern Art in New York thrust her into international prominence — and into a fashion craze, as her imagery was copied onto dresses and fabrics without her permission, an experience she found dismaying. She insisted, then and since, that her art was a serious investigation of perception, not decorative pattern.

biography.heading: The turn to colour
biography.text:
From the late 1960s Riley introduced colour, embarking on decades of research into how hues interact, advance and recede, and generate light and movement when set in carefully calibrated stripes and curves. Her stripe paintings and later her sequences of coloured discs and diagonals explore how the eye mixes adjacent colours and how a composition can seem to breathe and glow.

Travel to Egypt in 1979–80 was decisive: the colours of ancient Egyptian art inspired what she called her "Egyptian palette," a set of warm and cool hues she deployed in vertical stripes. Later she developed lozenge and curve motifs, continually testing how abstract colour and form act directly on perception. Throughout, her method remained rigorous: she works out compositions through extensive studies and employs assistants to execute the final, immaculate surfaces.

biography.heading: Method and ideas
biography.text:
Riley's practice is one of sustained, almost scientific inquiry. She does not paint to express emotion directly but to orchestrate visual events — to make the act of looking itself the content of the work. The viewer's eye and nervous system complete the painting; the energy and movement exist not on the canvas but in perception.

She has written and spoken with great clarity about her aims, aligning herself with a classical tradition that stretches from Seurat back to the Renaissance. For Riley, abstraction is not a rejection of nature but a way of capturing the dynamism of seeing — the pleasure, instability and intensity of vision.

biography.heading: Recognition and legacy
biography.text:
In 1968 Riley became the first woman, and the first British painter, to win the International Prize for Painting at the Venice Biennale. She has since been the subject of major retrospectives at institutions including the Tate and the Hayward Gallery, and has created large wall paintings for public and museum spaces.

Now regarded as one of the most important British painters of her era, Riley has pursued a single, deepening investigation with extraordinary consistency for more than sixty years. Her influence extends across abstraction, design and the broader culture, and her work remains a touchstone for any artist concerned with colour, perception and the active role of the viewer.

name: Felipe Pantone
artStatement: Pantone fuses graffiti, kinetic art and digital aesthetics into hard-edged works of gradient colour, glitch and optical pattern — a visual language for the speed, screens and dynamism of the hyper-digital age.

headshots.credit: Felipe Pantone — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: A Kinetic Wall Sculpture by Felipe Pantone
From Graffiti to Design through Contemporary Art

biography.heading: From graffiti to studio
biography.text:
Felipe Pantone was born in 1986 in Argentina and raised in Spain, settling in the coastal city of Torrevalencia. He began writing graffiti as a child, around the age of twelve, immersing himself in the codes, letterforms and adrenaline of street painting. That early grounding in graffiti — its speed, its public scale, its love of bold colour and dynamic form — remains the foundation of everything he does.

He later trained in fine art in Valencia, bridging the worlds of the street and the gallery. Out of this dual formation emerged a singular visual language that fuses the energy of graffiti with the heritage of kinetic and Op art and the aesthetics of the digital age.

biography.heading: A language of the digital age
biography.text:
Pantone's work looks unmistakably contemporary: vivid gradients, glitch patterns, optical grids, chromatic spectrums, QR-like codes and forms that seem to be buffering, pixelating or in motion. He treats the visual noise of screens, data and the internet as raw material, translating the look of the digital world into paintings, sculptures and murals.

His recurring motifs — the full RGB and CMYK colour spectrum, black-and-white optical patterns, arrows and grids — speak to a world of acceleration, connectivity and constant flux. Series with names like Chromadynamica, Subtractive Variability and Optichromie investigate how colour and pattern behave under conditions of speed and change, updating the concerns of mid-century kinetic art for an era of pixels and bandwidth.

biography.heading: Kinetic and interactive works
biography.text:
Pantone has increasingly made literally moving, interactive work. His kinetic wall sculptures use motorised, rotating glass or panels printed with colour gradients, so that the piece endlessly recombines into shifting bands of colour — a hypnotic, ever-changing artwork that never repeats. He has also built installations and machines that invite participation, extending the kinetic tradition of Soto, Cruz-Diez and Le Parc into a new technological context.

This embrace of movement, mechanism and interaction is central to his project. Like his kinetic forebears, he wants the artwork to be activated in real time — but his vocabulary belongs to the smartphone and the gradient tool rather than the mid-century optical lab.

biography.heading: Murals and public scale
biography.text:
Pantone remains, at heart, a muralist, and he has painted vast, dazzling walls in cities around the world. His large-scale public works bring his electric palettes and optical patterns to building façades and urban spaces, retaining the ambition and accessibility of his graffiti origins. He often works at architectural scale, wrapping entire structures in colour and pattern.

He collaborates widely across design, fashion and industry, and his aesthetic has proved enormously influential on contemporary visual culture. Yet the murals keep his practice anchored in the public realm, where his work can be encountered freely by anyone passing by.

biography.heading: Significance
biography.text:
Still relatively young, Pantone has become one of the most recognisable artists working at the intersection of graffiti, kinetic art and digital culture, exhibiting internationally and developing a devoted global following. His work is collected, widely reproduced and instantly identifiable.

His contribution is to have given the venerable traditions of optical and kinetic abstraction a thoroughly twenty-first-century form — translating the textures of data, screens and acceleration into vivid, joyful, physically present art. In doing so he connects the street, the museum and the network, and demonstrates that the investigation of colour and movement begun decades ago remains vital and open.

name: Tauba Auerbach
artStatement: Auerbach works across painting, sculpture, weaving and artists' books to explore structure, dimension and perception — fold paintings, woven canvases and pattern systems that probe how order, space and logic are constructed.

headshots.credit: Portrait

videos.title: Tauba Auerbach in “Bodies of Knowledge” — Art21

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Tauba Auerbach was born in San Francisco in 1981 and studied at Stanford University. Before fully entering the art world they worked as a sign painter, an experience that instilled a deep respect for craft, lettering and the mechanics of how images are made — concerns that run throughout their work. Auerbach, who uses they/them pronouns, came to prominence in New York in the late 2000s and 2010s.

From the start, Auerbach's art has been driven by a fascination with systems of order: language, mathematics, logic, geometry and the structures that underlie perception and matter. They approach these subjects with both intellectual rigour and a playful, sensuous attention to surface and material.

biography.heading: Language, logic and the limits of systems
biography.text:
Auerbach's early work explored language and binary systems — paintings and works on paper that scrambled the alphabet, visualised logical operations, or pushed typography to the edge of legibility. These pieces probe how meaning is built from arbitrary signs, and what happens when ordered systems are stressed, exhausted or made to break down.

This interest in the threshold between order and chaos, sense and nonsense, has remained central. Auerbach is drawn to the points where a rational structure becomes strange — where a grid warps, a code fails to resolve, or a mathematical idea produces something genuinely uncanny. The work treats abstraction as a way of thinking about the deep structures of reality.

biography.heading: The Fold paintings
biography.text:
Auerbach's best-known works are the Fold paintings. To make them, they crumple or fold a canvas, then spray it with paint from different angles so that the creases catch the pigment, before stretching the canvas flat again. The result is a flat surface that appears, astonishingly, to be a three-dimensional crumpled or folded plane — a trompe-l'oeil of fabric and shadow that confuses the eye's reading of depth and surface.

The Fold series perfectly encapsulates Auerbach's concerns: it records a real physical action (the folding) as a frozen trace, plays with dimensionality, and turns a simple process into a meditation on how we perceive space. The paintings are at once conceptually precise and visually seductive.

biography.heading: Across media: weaving, glass and books
biography.text:
Auerbach works far beyond painting. They have made intricate woven works, marbled surfaces, glasswork, sculpture and musical and mechanical devices, and they run a small press, Diagonal Press, producing artist's books, type specimens and printed matter. A recurring interest in the structures of weaving and in three-dimensional geometry — knots, lattices, mathematical surfaces — connects these diverse outputs.

A major mid-career survey at the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, titled S v Z, brought together this wide-ranging practice and underscored the underlying coherence: across every medium, Auerbach is investigating structure, dimension and the hidden order of things.

biography.heading: Standing
biography.text:
Auerbach is widely regarded as one of the most inventive American artists of their generation, exhibiting internationally and represented in major collections. Their work bridges conceptual art and a deep love of craft, and refuses easy categorisation.

The enduring achievement is a body of work that makes the abstract structures of mathematics, language and matter visible and tangible — and beautiful. By folding a canvas or scrambling an alphabet, Auerbach turns the architecture of reality into objects of wonder, inviting the viewer to feel the strangeness that lurks within order itself.

name: Sarah Morris
artStatement: Morris makes glossy geometric paintings and films that map the psychology of cities, institutions and power — bright grids and abstracted architecture mirrored by documentary-essay films on urban systems.

headshots.credit: Painting by Sarah Morris — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Sarah Morris in Conversation with Isabelle Graw

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Sarah Morris was born in 1967 in Sevenoaks, England, and grew up between the United Kingdom and the United States. She studied at Brown University and at the Whitney Independent Study Program in New York, absorbing a strongly theoretical, conceptual approach to art. She is based between New York and London and works in two intertwined media: painting and film.

Morris's art is concerned with the codes, psychologies and power structures of the modern city. She treats architecture, corporate identity, bureaucracy and urban life as systems to be mapped and decoded, producing a body of work that is at once coolly abstract and densely loaded with social meaning.

biography.heading: The paintings: cities as abstraction
biography.text:
Morris is best known for large, glossy geometric paintings built from grids, bands and interlocking polygons in bright, hard-edged colour. Executed in high-gloss household paint, their reflective surfaces and crisp lines evoke glass curtain-walls, lobbies, signage and the dazzle of urban modernity.

Though abstract, the paintings are tied to specific places and systems. Series have taken as their subjects cities such as Los Angeles, Las Vegas, Washington, Beijing and Rio de Janeiro, as well as institutions and industries. The geometry distils the visual and psychological logic of these environments — the architecture of power, money and spectacle — into shimmering, seductive surfaces that are never merely decorative.

biography.heading: The films
biography.text:
Running parallel to the paintings is an equally important body of films. Without narration or interviews in the conventional sense, Morris's films assemble hypnotic montages of cities and institutions — the rhythms of Beijing during the 2008 Olympics, the machinery of Los Angeles, the corridors of finance and government. They reveal the hidden choreography of modern systems: how power moves, how cities function, how spectacle is produced.

The films and paintings illuminate one another. The films provide the observational, documentary engine of her practice, while the paintings crystallise its findings into abstract form. Together they constitute a sustained portrait of contemporary urban capitalism.

biography.heading: Method and ideas
biography.text:
Morris's work is grounded in a fascination with typologies and structures — the recurring forms of corporate architecture, the protocols of bureaucracy, the semiotics of branding. She is influenced by theory and by filmmakers and modernist architects, and she approaches the city as a kind of text to be read.

Her cool, polished aesthetic carries a critical charge: the beauty of her surfaces is inseparable from a clear-eyed analysis of control, surveillance and desire in the built environment. The viewer is seduced by colour and geometry while being shown the underlying mechanics of modern power.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Morris has exhibited widely at major museums and galleries internationally, including significant survey exhibitions such as her presentation at the Deichtorhallen in Hamburg, and her work is held in important public collections. She is recognised as a leading figure in the dialogue between abstraction and the analysis of contemporary society.
