Her lasting contribution is a distinctive fusion of high-modernist abstraction with documentary inquiry — a body of paintings and films that decode the psychology of the modern metropolis. In Morris's work, the gleaming geometry of the city is revealed as both an object of pleasure and a map of power.

name: Julio Le Parc
artStatement: A founder of kinetic and op art and of the Groupe de Recherche d'Art Visuel (GRAV), Le Parc made works of moving light, reflection and instability designed to activate the spectator and dissolve the authority of the static art object.

headshots.credit: Julio Le Parc — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: Grand Prize for Painting, Venice Biennale (1966)

videos.title: Julio Le Parc at the Serpentine Sackler Gallery
Optimism, Forever! The World of Julio Le Parc

biography.heading: Argentine roots and Paris
biography.text:
Julio Le Parc was born in 1928 in Mendoza, Argentina, into modest circumstances. He studied at the National School of Fine Arts in Buenos Aires, where he became involved in debates about abstraction and the social role of art. In 1958 he moved to Paris, which became the base for a long and influential career.

In Paris, Le Parc was at the heart of the kinetic art movement. In 1960 he co-founded the Groupe de Recherche d'Art Visuel (GRAV), a collective of artists committed to experimental, participatory work that broke down the boundary between artist and spectator. GRAV's collaborative ethos and its emphasis on instability, perception and play shaped Le Parc's entire practice.

biography.heading: Light and movement
biography.text:
Le Parc's central material is light. He creates works in which light is reflected, refracted and set in motion — small mobile elements, polished metal surfaces and mirrors that scatter shifting patterns across walls and rooms. His Continuel-lumière works fill darkened spaces with restless, dancing reflections, turning the gallery into an immersive, ever-changing environment.

He also makes works that exploit optical illusion and visual instability: surfaces that vibrate and dazzle, structures that seem to move as the viewer shifts position. Across all of these, his aim is to activate perception and to give the spectator a dynamic, bodily experience rather than a fixed object to contemplate.

biography.heading: Participation and play
biography.text:
Le Parc has always insisted that art should engage and even destabilise the viewer, dissolving the passive, reverent relationship of the traditional museum. With GRAV he created labyrinths and interactive environments full of games, distorting surfaces, unstable floors and devices to be manipulated, designed to surprise and activate ordinary people.

This commitment to participation has a political dimension. Le Parc, a lifelong leftist, saw the democratisation of aesthetic experience — art that anyone could activate and enjoy, free of elite codes — as part of a broader emancipatory project. Famously, when he won the grand prize for painting at the 1966 Venice Biennale, his subversive, anti-establishment stance accompanied the honour.

biography.heading: Colour and continuity
biography.text:
Alongside the light works, Le Parc has pursued a long investigation of colour, developing systematic sequences based on a fixed set of hues arranged in shifting progressions. These vibrant, modular colour works — paintings, reliefs and immense installations — share the kinetic concern with movement and perception, now realised through chromatic rhythm rather than literal motion.

Remarkably prolific into his nineties, Le Parc has continued to produce immersive, joyful, optically intense environments, bridging the historic kinetic moment of the 1960s and the contemporary appetite for participatory, experiential art.

biography.heading: Legacy
biography.text:
Le Parc is recognised as one of the founding figures of kinetic and Op art and a pioneer of participatory installation. His work has been the subject of major retrospectives, including presentations at the Pérez Art Museum Miami, the Serpentine Galleries in London and elsewhere, and is held in important collections worldwide.

His enduring significance lies in his vision of art as a shared, activating experience — light and movement offered to everyone, on equal terms. In linking aesthetic experiment to a democratic ideal, Le Parc helped redefine the relationship between artwork and viewer for the second half of the twentieth century and beyond.

name: Jim Lambie
artStatement: Lambie turns everyday objects and pop culture into vivid sculptural installations, most famously his 'Zobop' floors of coloured vinyl tape that warp and energise architectural space.

headshots.credit: Jim Lambie — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background in music and Glasgow
biography.text:
Jim Lambie was born in Glasgow in 1964. Before becoming a full-time artist he was deeply involved in music, playing in bands and working as a DJ, and that immersion in pop, rhythm and club culture is fundamental to his work. He studied at the Glasgow School of Art in the early 1990s, emerging from a celebrated Glasgow scene that produced a generation of internationally successful artists.

Music remains the key to Lambie's sensibility. His work has the energy, colour and immediacy of pop and the dance floor, and he often speaks of wanting his art to have the visceral, mood-altering effect of a great record. He turns the everyday detritus of life — record sleeves, posters, handbags, belts, buttons, mirrors — into vibrant sculptures and installations.

biography.heading: Zobop
biography.text:
Lambie's signature work is Zobop, a floor installation made from coloured vinyl tape. Beginning at the edges of a room and working inward, he lays parallel lines of tape that follow the contours of the space, so that the entire floor becomes a pulsing field of concentric, rhythmic colour. Each Zobop is unique to its room, transforming an ordinary gallery floor into a dazzling, almost psychedelic environment.

First made in the late 1990s, Zobop has been installed in museums around the world, including the Museum of Modern Art in New York. It is at once rigorous and exuberant — geometric and site-specific, yet immediately joyful — and it perfectly captures Lambie's gift for turning a simple material and a simple gesture into an overwhelming sensory experience.

biography.heading: Sculpture from the everyday
biography.text:
Beyond Zobop, Lambie makes sculptures from found and consumer objects, often coated in bright gloss paint or mirror. He has made works from chrome-plated handbags, doors studded with buttons, ladders threaded with objects, and potatoes dipped in colourful paint. Mirrored surfaces recur, fragmenting reflections and pulling the surroundings into the work.

These sculptures share the pop sensibility of the floors: they take the cast-offs of ordinary life and charge them with glamour, colour and a sense of the night. There is a consistent alchemy at work — the transformation of the cheap, the discarded and the everyday into something seductive and strange.

biography.heading: Colour, rhythm and space
biography.text:
Lambie's art is fundamentally about colour, rhythm and the activation of space. Whether through the optical pulse of a taped floor or the gleam of a painted object, he aims to alter the feel of an environment and the mood of the viewer, much as music does. His installations are immersive and bodily; you do not just look at them, you stand inside them.

This synaesthetic quality — the translation of musical energy into visual form — distinguishes his work and connects it to a long tradition of artists fascinated by the relationship between sound, colour and sensation.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Lambie was shortlisted for the Turner Prize in 2005 and has exhibited extensively in major museums and galleries internationally. His Zobop floors in particular have become iconic, instantly recognisable contributions to contemporary installation art.

His lasting achievement is a body of work that brings the immediacy and joy of pop music into the visual arts, turning everyday materials and ordinary spaces into electric, immersive experiences. Few artists have so successfully captured the feeling of colour and rhythm as pure, infectious pleasure.

name: Spencer Finch
artStatement: Finch records the colour, light and atmosphere of specific places and moments, translating fugitive perceptual experience into installations of filtered light, glass and fluorescent tubes.

headshots.credit: Work by Spencer Finch — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: Trying to Remember the Color of the Sky on That September Morning

videos.title: Tour of Spencer Finch’s Back to Kansas
Spencer Finch: The Skies Can't Keep Their Secret

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Spencer Finch was born in 1962 in New Haven, Connecticut. He studied at Hamilton College and earned a graduate degree from the Rhode Island School of Design. Based in Brooklyn, he has built a distinctive practice devoted to one of the most elusive subjects in art: the precise, subjective experience of light, colour and perception in a particular place and moment.

Finch's work sits at the meeting point of art and science, but it is animated by poetry and memory rather than cold measurement. He is fascinated by the impossibility of ever fully capturing a sensory experience — the exact colour of light at a specific time and place — and by the beautiful, instructive failures that result from trying.

biography.heading: Capturing the light of a moment
biography.text:
Much of Finch's work begins with an act of careful observation: he measures and records the colour and quality of light at a specific site and time — sunlight on a particular morning, moonlight over a lake, the light in a historic room — using tools like colorimeters, and then attempts to recreate that exact sensation using lamps, filters, fluorescent tubes, watercolour or coloured glass.

The results range from arrays of carefully gelled fluorescent fixtures to delicate watercolours and installations of coloured filters. Each is, in effect, a portrait of a moment of perception. Because such an experience can never be perfectly reproduced, the works carry a poignant sense of striving and approximation — they are about the gap between experience and representation as much as about light itself.

biography.heading: Memory, literature and place
biography.text:
Finch's work is steeped in literary and historical reference. He has made pieces inspired by Emily Dickinson, by Proust, by classical literature and by specific historical sites, using light and colour to evoke states of memory and feeling. The titles and premises of his works often read like small poems, framing a scientific procedure within a literary and emotional context.

This fusion of empirical method and literary sensibility gives his art its particular flavour: rigorous yet tender, conceptual yet deeply felt. He treats colour and light as carriers of memory and mood, and his installations invite a slow, attentive, reflective mode of looking.

biography.heading: Public works and major commissions
biography.text:
Finch has created significant public and architectural commissions. Among the most celebrated is his installation at the National September 11 Memorial Museum in New York, Trying to Remember the Color of the Sky on That September Morning — a wall of individually hand-painted blue paper squares, each a different shade, attempting to recall the precise blue of the sky on the morning of the attacks. It is a deeply moving meditation on memory, loss and the unreliability of recollection.

Other major works include large-scale window and glass commissions and installations in museums and public buildings, where his coloured-light environments transform architecture into an experience of perception and atmosphere.

biography.heading: Standing
biography.text:
Finch has exhibited widely in the United States and internationally and is represented in major collections. He is recognised as one of the most thoughtful contemporary artists working with light, colour and perception, distinguished by the literary and emotional depth he brings to a quasi-scientific practice.

His enduring contribution is to have made the fleeting, private experience of light into a subject worthy of sustained artistic attention — and to have shown that the attempt to capture such experience, however doomed, can produce works of great beauty and feeling. His art turns measurement into elegy.

name: Anthony McCall
artStatement: McCall pioneered 'solid light' works in which a film projector throws slowly evolving lines of light through haze, sculpting volumetric forms that visitors can walk into — film, sculpture, drawing and time collapsed into a single beam.

headshots.credit: Anthony McCall — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Solid Light Films and Other Works (1971–2014)

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Anthony McCall was born in 1946 in St Paul's Cray, Kent, England. He came of age in the experimental film and performance scene of London in the late 1960s and early 1970s, before moving to New York in 1973. His work occupies a singular territory at the intersection of cinema, sculpture and drawing, and he is the originator of what he calls "solid light" films.

Trained in graphic design and active in the avant-garde, McCall was interested from the start in the basic physical conditions of cinema — not the images projected, but the beam of light itself, travelling through space. This led him to one of the most original ideas in post-war art.

biography.heading: Line Describing a Cone and solid light
biography.text:
In 1973 McCall made Line Describing a Cone, the founding work of his career and a landmark of experimental film. Over thirty minutes, a projector casts a single point of light that slowly grows into a complete circle. In a haze-filled darkened room, the projected beam becomes visible as a three-dimensional volume — a cone of light hanging in space that viewers can walk around, reach into and inhabit.

The "film" is not something on a screen; it is the beam itself, sculpted in light and air. This radical insight — that projected light could be experienced as a solid, navigable form — collapsed the distinction between cinema and sculpture, and gave rise to an entire body of work. Audiences became participants, moving through the work and casting shadows into the light.

biography.heading: Disappearance and return
biography.text:
The solid-light works depended on atmospheric haze to make the beam visible. In the 1970s this came partly from cigarette smoke in galleries; as smoking indoors disappeared and his living circumstances changed, McCall largely stopped exhibiting, and for roughly two decades he worked in graphic design and was little known in the art world.

His rediscovery in the early 2000s, aided by the advent of reliable haze machines and digital projection, prompted a remarkable second act. He resumed and expanded the solid-light series, now using computers to control slowly evolving forms and orienting some works vertically, so that blades and veils of light descend from ceiling to floor. The work reached vast new audiences in major museums.

biography.heading: The mature installations
biography.text:
McCall's recent solid-light installations are immersive and meditative. Slowly mutating geometries of light — curves, waves, ellipses — unfold over long durations in darkened, hazy rooms, and visitors wander among the luminous membranes, breaking and shaping them with their bodies. The works hover between drawing (the moving lines), film (the temporal unfolding) and sculpture (the volumes in space).

These pieces have a quiet, almost spiritual power. They reward patience and presence, drawing the viewer into a contemplative encounter with the simplest and most fundamental material of cinema and vision: light moving through space and time.

biography.heading: Significance
biography.text:
McCall has been the subject of major exhibitions internationally, including significant presentations at institutions such as Tate, and his work is held in leading collections. He is recognised as a pioneer of expanded cinema and a singular figure whose work bridges film, sculpture and installation.

His enduring achievement is to have discovered an entirely new artistic medium — solid light — and to have sustained and deepened it across half a century. In turning the projector's beam into a thing you can walk through, McCall created some of the most original and affecting immersive art of his time.

name: Doug Wheeler
artStatement: A founder of the Light and Space movement, Wheeler builds 'infinity environments' — seamless, evenly lit rooms that erase edges and horizon so that light, space and silence become the entire content of the work.

headshots.credit: Portrait

biography.heading: Background and the California light
biography.text:
Doug Wheeler was born in 1939 in Globe, Arizona. The vast, luminous skies of the American Southwest and his early experiences flying small planes with his father shaped a lifelong sensitivity to light, space and atmosphere. He studied in Los Angeles and became, in the 1960s, one of the central figures of the Light and Space movement that flourished in Southern California.

Alongside artists such as James Turrell and Robert Irwin, Wheeler turned away from the art object toward the conditions of perception itself — light, space and the subtle thresholds of seeing. His ambition was nothing less than to make light and space the entire substance of the work, dissolving the boundaries of the room and of the self.

biography.heading: Light-encasement and the vanishing edge
biography.text:
Wheeler's early breakthrough came with his "light encasement" works of the late 1960s: painted, vacuum-formed panels, sometimes incorporating hidden neon, whose edges seemed to dissolve so that the surface appeared to glow and float free of the wall. He meticulously controlled the environment around these works, rounding the corners of rooms and coving the walls so that the architecture itself disappeared.

This pursuit of the dissolved edge and the boundless field is the through-line of his career. He sought to remove every cue that would let the eye fix a surface or a limit, producing experiences of pure, edgeless luminosity in which the viewer loses any sense of where the room ends.

biography.heading: Infinity environments
biography.text:
Wheeler is best known for his immersive "infinity environments": rooms with curved, seamless white surfaces, carefully graded artificial and sometimes natural light, in which all sense of depth, corner and boundary evaporates. Standing inside, the viewer floats in a softly glowing, apparently limitless space, unable to judge distance — an experience that can be disorienting, serene and profound.

These environments, which he conceived early but was often only able to realise decades later as technology and resources allowed, represent the fullest expression of his vision. A celebrated installation at David Zwirner in New York finally brought one of his long-planned infinity rooms to a wide public, confirming his importance.

biography.heading: Light and sound; patience and precision
biography.text:
Some of Wheeler's environments incorporate subtle gradations that mimic the slow changes of natural daylight, and he has explored the relationship between perceptual emptiness and heightened awareness, sometimes likening the experience to the silence of remote desert landscapes he knew as a young man.

His practice is defined by extraordinary precision and patience. Achieving a truly edgeless, immersive field requires exacting control of surface, lighting and architecture, and many of his most ambitious ideas waited years or decades for the means to realise them. The works reward a slow, attentive surrender on the part of the viewer.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Long under-recognised relative to some of his Light and Space peers, Wheeler has in recent decades been celebrated as a foundational figure of the movement, with major gallery installations and inclusion in important historical surveys of California art.

His enduring contribution is to have pushed the dematerialisation of the art object about as far as it can go — to the point where the "work" is simply light, space and the viewer's own perception. His infinity environments offer a rare, almost meditative encounter with the limits of seeing, and stand among the purest realisations of the Light and Space ideal.

name: Do Ho Suh
artStatement: Suh recreates the architecture of homes he has lived in as translucent, full-scale fabric sculptures — measuring memory, migration and the idea of home as a portable, ghostly space stitched in colored gossamer.

headshots.credit: Do Ho Suh — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Do Ho Suh was born in 1962 in Seoul, South Korea, the son of a celebrated traditional Korean painter. He grew up steeped in Korean art and architecture, and trained in Oriental painting at Seoul National University before fulfilling mandatory military service. In the early 1990s he moved to the United States to study sculpture, earning degrees at the Rhode Island School of Design and Yale, and he has since lived between Seoul, New York and London.

This experience of migration — of repeatedly moving between cultures and homes — is the central subject of Suh's art. He is preoccupied with space, memory, identity and belonging, and above all with the meaning of "home": the places we inhabit, carry with us and reconstruct in memory.

biography.heading: Fabric architecture
biography.text:
Suh is best known for full-scale recreations of his homes and the spaces he has lived in, sewn from translucent, brightly coloured fabric. Using gossamer-thin polyester and meticulous tailoring, he reconstructs entire apartments, corridors, staircases, doorways and even individual fixtures — light switches, radiators, door handles, toilets — as ghostly, weightless, see-through replicas.

These fabric architectures are uncanny and deeply moving. Familiar domestic spaces become diaphanous, portable and dreamlike, able to be folded into a suitcase and rebuilt anywhere. They embody the condition of the migrant, for whom home is not a fixed place but something carried, remembered and continually reassembled. The translucency makes the spaces feel like memories — present yet immaterial.

biography.heading: Measuring memory
biography.text:
Suh's precision is extraordinary: he measures every detail of a remembered space and reproduces it exactly in fabric, so that the works are at once intensely personal and almost architectural in their accuracy. Pieces such as his fabric staircases and the "Hubs" — connective passageways between rooms — map the thresholds and in-between spaces that structure a life lived across continents.

He has also made works in other media that pursue similar themes, including rubbings of the surfaces of his former homes and sculptures composed of thousands of tiny figures, exploring the relationship between the individual and the collective, the personal and the shared.

biography.heading: The individual and the multitude
biography.text:
A recurring concern in Suh's work is the tension between the single person and the mass. In some sculptures, vast numbers of miniature figures collectively support a structure or form a surface, raising questions about anonymity, society and the place of the individual within larger systems — questions rooted partly in his experience of military service and of densely populated Seoul.

This interest complements the domestic works: where the fabric homes address the intimate, interior experience of space and memory, the multitude works address the social and collective dimensions of identity. Together they form a sustained meditation on how individuals inhabit both private and public worlds.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Suh represented Korea at the Venice Biennale and has exhibited at major museums worldwide, including a significant survey, Walk the House, at Tate Modern. His work is held in leading international collections and has made him one of the most admired Korean artists of his generation.

His enduring contribution is to have given poignant, beautiful form to the experience of displacement and the meaning of home in a globalised world. By rebuilding his homes in translucent fabric, Suh transforms private memory into shared experience, and offers a profound reflection on belonging, mobility and the spaces that shape who we are.

name: Rachel Whiteread
artStatement: Whiteread casts the negative space in and around everyday things — the air inside a room, beneath a chair, within a water tower — turning absence, memory and the overlooked into solid, monumental form.

headshots.credit: Rachel Whiteread, 2018 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

awards: CBE / DBE for services to art

videos.title: Rachel Whiteread in Conversation with Ann Gallagher
Rachel Whiteread: Artists Reflect on What Is Happening

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Rachel Whiteread was born in London in 1963 to a creative, politically engaged family; her mother was an artist. She studied painting at Brighton Polytechnic and then sculpture at the Slade School of Fine Art in London, emerging in the late 1980s as part of the generation that became known as the Young British Artists. She quickly distinguished herself through a singular sculptural idea that she has pursued with remarkable consistency ever since.

Whiteread's art is built on the technique of casting — but rather than casting objects, she casts the empty space around and inside them. She makes solid the void: the space beneath a chair, inside a room, under a bed, within a hot-water bottle. This simple, profound inversion gives physical form to absence and to the overlooked negative spaces of everyday life.

biography.heading: Casting the void
biography.text:
Whiteread's early works cast the spaces associated with intimate domestic objects — the underside of a bath, the inside of a wardrobe, the space beneath a table. These pieces, often in plaster, rubber or resin, are quiet, ghostly and charged with memory and human presence, evoking the bodies and lives that once used these things while rendering the objects themselves absent.

Her materials — plaster, concrete, resin, rubber — and her muted, mortuary palette give the works a sense of stillness and elegy. They are about time, memory, absence and the traces that human life leaves in the spaces it occupies. A cast of the air inside a room becomes a memorial to everything that happened there.

biography.heading: House and the public monument
biography.text:
In 1993 Whiteread created House, one of the most celebrated and controversial public artworks in British history: a solid concrete cast of the entire interior of a condemned Victorian terraced house in London's East End, left standing after the original building was demolished around it. This monumental, melancholy block — a memorial to a vanished home and a vanishing way of life — won her the Turner Prize that year, making her the first woman to receive it, even as it provoked fierce public debate and was eventually demolished.

House announced Whiteread as a sculptor of public memory. She went on to make permanent monuments, most notably the Holocaust Memorial in Vienna's Judenplatz, a cast of the inside of a library with its books turned outward — a powerful image of absence, loss and the unread lives of the murdered.

biography.heading: Scale, materials and stillness
biography.text:
Whiteread has worked across an enormous range of scale, from small casts of the spaces inside everyday objects to vast architectural works, including a translucent resin cast of the space beneath a water tower in New York and a monumental plinth commission for Trafalgar Square. She has also made casts of huts, sheds, staircases and floors, continuing her exploration of architecture and domestic space.

Whatever the scale, her work is defined by stillness, restraint and a meditative attention to the spaces we ordinarily ignore. By solidifying emptiness, she makes us newly aware of presence and absence, memory and loss, and the silent eloquence of ordinary places.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Whiteread represented Britain at the Venice Biennale and has been honoured with major retrospectives, including a comprehensive survey at Tate Britain, and with national honours. She is regarded as one of the most significant British sculptors of her generation.

Her enduring achievement is to have made absence visible. Through the patient casting of voids, she has created a body of work that speaks quietly but profoundly about memory, mortality and the human traces embedded in the spaces of everyday life. Few artists have built so much from a single, deceptively simple idea.

name: Olga de Amaral
artStatement: De Amaral expands weaving into architecture and landscape, building large-scale fiber works layered with gesso, gold and silver leaf that hang in space and shimmer between textile, painting and pre-Columbian heritage.

headshots.credit: Olga de Amaral — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Olga de Amaral, Colombian Textile Artist

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Olga de Amaral was born in 1932 in Bogotá, Colombia. She initially trained in architectural design before studying at Cranbrook Academy of Art in Michigan in the mid-1950s, where she learned weaving and textile art. Returning to Colombia, she became a pioneering figure in the international fiber art movement that flourished from the 1960s onward, elevating textile from craft to ambitious contemporary art.

De Amaral's work is rooted in Colombian identity, landscape and history, fused with a modernist sense of structure and abstraction. Over more than six decades she has developed a singular body of woven works that hang in space like luminous, monumental tapestries — at once sculpture, painting and textile.

biography.heading: Weaving as structure
biography.text:
De Amaral works primarily with fiber — linen, cotton, horsehair — which she weaves, knots, wraps and layers into large, three-dimensional structures. Rejecting the flat, framed picture, her works are often free-hanging and architectural, occupying space and responding to light. The architectural training of her youth is evident in her command of structure, repetition and modular construction.

Her early works explored woven texture and form in earthy, natural tones; over time she developed increasingly complex, sculptural constructions that blur the line between two and three dimensions. Throughout, the labour and logic of weaving — the interlacing of threads into a coherent whole — remains both her method and her metaphor.

biography.heading: Gold and the luminous surface
biography.text:
De Amaral is most celebrated for her use of gold and metallic leaf. From the 1970s she began applying gesso and gold or palladium leaf to her woven surfaces, creating shimmering, light-catching works that evoke both the gold of pre-Columbian art and the gilded altarpieces of colonial Latin American churches. These radiant pieces seem to glow from within and transform with the movement of the viewer and the changing light.

The gold connects her work to deep layers of Colombian and Andean cultural memory — to the legendary goldwork of indigenous civilisations and to the syncretic religious art of the colonial era. Her surfaces are at once abstract and richly resonant, carrying the weight of history while remaining thoroughly contemporary.

biography.heading: Landscape, memory and abstraction
biography.text:
Many of de Amaral's works evoke the Colombian landscape — its mountains, light, vegetation and geology — without depicting it literally. Series with titles referring to mountains, stelae, and natural elements translate the experience of place into abstract woven form and colour. Her work is a kind of textile memory of land, culture and the passage of time.

This grounding in place and history distinguishes her from the more purely formal strands of fiber art. For de Amaral, abstraction is a way of holding cultural and personal memory, and her materials and techniques carry meanings rooted in her Colombian identity.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Long admired within the fiber art world, de Amaral has in recent years received major international recognition, including a comprehensive retrospective at the Fondation Cartier in Paris and renewed attention from museums and collectors worldwide. Her work is held in major collections internationally.

Her enduring achievement is to have helped establish textile as a serious medium of contemporary art and to have created a body of work of extraordinary beauty and cultural depth. Through woven fiber and shimmering gold, de Amaral transforms thread into landscape, memory and light, bridging ancient tradition and modern abstraction.

name: Lee Ufan
artStatement: A leading figure of Japan's Mono-ha movement, Lee makes spare paintings and sculptures that stage relationships between natural stone, industrial steel and empty space — privileging encounter, process and the unmade over representation.

headshots.credit: Lee Ufan — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Lee Ufan: Why he works with stone and steel
Lee Ufan: Something Appears From Nothing
Touching Infinity: A Conversation with Lee Ufan

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Lee Ufan was born in 1936 in Haman, in what is now South Korea, and received a traditional education in calligraphy, poetry and painting. In 1956 he moved to Japan, where he studied philosophy at Nihon University, immersing himself in both Eastern thought and Western phenomenology. This dual grounding — in East Asian aesthetics and in European philosophy — underlies his entire practice as an artist and writer.

In the late 1960s and 1970s, Lee became the leading theorist and a central practitioner of Mono-ha ("School of Things"), a pivotal Japanese movement that, in parallel with arte povera and minimalism in the West, rejected the making of representational images in favour of presenting raw, natural and industrial materials in carefully considered relationships.

biography.heading: Mono-ha and the art of relations
biography.text:
Lee's sculptures, which he calls Relatum, typically bring together a few simple elements — a natural stone, a sheet or plate of industrial steel, sometimes glass or rubber — arranged in spare, deliberate configurations. Nothing is carved or transformed; the materials are presented as they are, and the "work" lies in the relationships between them and the surrounding space.

A stone resting on or beside a steel plate, for Lee, stages an encounter between nature and industry, the made and the found, presence and emptiness. The emphasis is on the space and tension between things, and on the viewer's heightened awareness of materials, site and the act of perception. This is an art of restraint, attention and relation rather than of statement or spectacle.

biography.heading: Painting and the single stroke
biography.text:
Lee is equally important as a painter. From the 1970s he developed series — From Point, From Line, and later Correspondence and Dialogue — in which he applies a loaded brush to the canvas in measured marks, often a single stroke or a small cluster of marks, allowing the pigment to fade as the brush empties. The painted mark exists in dialogue with the vast areas of bare canvas around it.

These paintings are meditations on the relationship between presence and absence, the made mark and the empty field. Drawing on his calligraphic training and Zen-influenced aesthetics, Lee charges emptiness with meaning: the unpainted space is as active and important as the mark itself. The discipline and breath of the gesture matter as much as the result.

biography.heading: Philosophy of emptiness
biography.text:
Lee is a prolific writer, and his art is inseparable from his philosophy. Influenced by phenomenology and by East Asian thought, he is concerned with encounter, with the limits of human making, and with an art that opens onto the world rather than imposing the artist's ego upon it. He values the unmade, the natural and the empty, seeking a humble, attentive relationship between human beings, objects and space.

This philosophical depth has made him a hugely influential figure, particularly in Korea and Japan but increasingly worldwide, as a thinker who articulated an alternative to the Western model of art as self-expression and object-making.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Lee has exhibited internationally at major institutions, with a notable retrospective at the Guggenheim in New York, permanent installations and dedicated spaces such as the Lee Ufan Museum on Naoshima in Japan and a museum in Busan, and major commissions around the world. He is recognised as one of the most important Asian artists of the post-war era.

His enduring contribution is a profound, restrained body of work — and a philosophy to match — that finds meaning in materials, space, emptiness and encounter. In an age of spectacle, Lee's art asks the viewer to slow down and attend to the quiet relationships between things, and to the charged emptiness in which they exist.

name: Tatsuo Miyajima
artStatement: Miyajima fills space with grids of digital LED counters cycling 1 to 9 — never reaching zero — drawing on Buddhist thought to make installations about time, life, death and continuous change.

headshots.credit: Tatsuo Miyajima, Life (Rhizome) — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Tatsuo Miyajima was born in 1957 in Tokyo. He studied at Tokyo University of the Arts, initially working in painting and performance before finding, in the 1980s, the medium that would define his career: the digital LED counter. Since then he has built an internationally significant body of work using glowing electronic numbers, informed throughout by Buddhist philosophy and a deep meditation on time, life and death.

Miyajima's work translates profound spiritual and philosophical ideas into a strikingly contemporary, technological language. His glowing arrays of counting numbers are at once cutting-edge and ancient in their concerns, fusing the aesthetics of the electronic age with the wisdom traditions of Asia.

biography.heading: Numbers that count life
biography.text:
Miyajima's signature works use LED displays that continuously count, typically from 1 to 9 (or 9 to 1), then go dark before beginning again — pointedly never showing zero. For Miyajima, the counting represents the cycle of life, and the dark interval between cycles stands for death and the void from which new life emerges. The absence of zero reflects his Buddhist conviction that there is no true nothingness, only continual transformation.

Each counter often runs at its own speed, set by the artist or by participants, so that an installation of hundreds or thousands of numbers becomes a shimmering field of individual lives, each with its own rhythm — being born, ageing and passing in endless variation. The work becomes a vast, glowing meditation on impermanence and the simultaneity of countless separate existences.

biography.heading: Three principles
biography.text:
Miyajima distils his philosophy into three core concepts that guide his art: "Keep Changing," "Connect with Everything," and "Continue Forever." These principles, drawn from Buddhist thought, express his view of reality as ceaseless change, universal interdependence and endless continuity — and they are made literal in the perpetual, varied, interconnected counting of his LEDs.

This framework gives his diverse works a deep coherence. Whether a single counter or a room-filling constellation of thousands, each piece embodies the same vision of a universe in constant flux, where everything is connected and life continues without end through perpetual transformation.

biography.heading: Scale, participation and site
biography.text:
Miyajima's installations range from intimate works to immense environments. He has filled darkened rooms with grids and towers of counting numbers, created works that descend like waterfalls of digits down the façades of buildings, and made participatory pieces in which members of the public set the speed of individual counters, embedding their own sense of life's tempo into the work.

He has also undertaken socially engaged projects connecting his art to communities and to histories of trauma and peace, reflecting the ethical dimension of his Buddhist worldview. Across all of these, the glowing number remains his fundamental unit — a simple, universal sign made to carry the largest questions of existence.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Miyajima rose to international prominence with appearances at major exhibitions including the Venice Biennale and documenta, and his work is held in leading collections and installed permanently at sites around the world. He is regarded as one of the most important Japanese artists of his generation.

His enduring achievement is to have created a wholly original visual language — the perpetually counting LED — capable of expressing the deepest themes of impermanence, interconnection and the cycle of life and death. In Miyajima's hands, the cool glow of digital technology becomes a vehicle for ancient wisdom and quiet spiritual reflection.

name: Chiharu Shiota
artStatement: Shiota weaves vast webs of red or black thread through entire rooms, entangling keys, beds, boats and dresses — externalizing memory, anxiety, connection and absence as immersive, body-scale drawings in space.

headshots.credit: Chiharu Shiota, The Soul Trembles — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Chiharu Shiota: The Soul Trembles, Paris
Chiharu Shiota & Mami Kataoka: Invisible Threads

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Chiharu Shiota was born in 1972 in Osaka, Japan. She studied painting before moving to Germany in the 1990s, where she studied under the performance artist Marina Abramović, among others, and settled in Berlin. The shift from painting to immersive, performative installation defined her career, and she has become one of the most internationally celebrated artists working with thread and space.

Shiota's art explores memory, anxiety, the body, relationships, life and death — universal themes drawn from her own experiences of dislocation, illness and loss. Her signature medium is yarn, which she weaves into vast, dense, room-filling webs that envelop everyday objects and entire spaces.

biography.heading: Weaving space with thread
biography.text:
Shiota is best known for monumental installations in which she strings thousands of metres of thread — most often black or blood red, sometimes white — across entire rooms, creating intricate, enveloping webs. Suspended within these networks are everyday objects charged with human presence: keys, beds, dresses, shoes, boats, chairs, letters.

The threads suggest connections and relationships — between people, between memories, between the living and the dead — as well as states of entanglement, anxiety and protection. Red thread, for Shiota, often evokes blood, the body and human bonds; black suggests the night sky, the universe and the unknown; white is associated with purity, beginnings and endings. Walking through these installations is an overwhelming, emotional experience of being inside a physical map of feeling and memory.

biography.heading: Objects and absence
biography.text:
The objects Shiota suspends in her webs are rarely her own; she often gathers used items — shoes, keys, letters — that carry the traces of unknown lives. A field of old shoes, each tagged with a handwritten memory, or a suspended burnt piano, becomes a meditation on absence, on the people who once owned and used these things, and on the persistence of memory in objects.

This concern with absence and presence runs throughout her work. The empty dress, the abandoned bed, the keys to unknown doors — all evoke human lives that are felt but not seen. Her installations are, in a sense, portraits of absence, giving form to what is gone.

biography.heading: Body, memory and the universe
biography.text:
Shiota's themes are deeply personal yet universal. Her own experiences — leaving Japan, living between cultures, confronting serious illness — inform a sustained inquiry into the body, mortality and the soul. Her major retrospective, The Soul Trembles, which has travelled the world, takes its title from the ineffable feeling she seeks to express: the trembling of the soul before life's largest questions.

She also works in performance, sculpture, drawing and video, but the immersive thread installations remain her most powerful statements — physical embodiments of the invisible networks of memory, emotion and connection that bind human existence.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Shiota represented Japan at the Venice Biennale in 2015 with a celebrated installation of keys suspended in red thread above wooden boats, and her touring retrospective The Soul Trembles has drawn enormous audiences across Asia, Europe and beyond. She is among the most popular and acclaimed installation artists working today.

Her enduring achievement is to have made the invisible visible — to give tangible, overwhelming form to memory, anxiety, connection and loss. Through the simple, obsessive act of weaving thread through space, Shiota creates environments that speak directly and profoundly to the deepest currents of human feeling.

name: Teresita Fernández
artStatement: Fernández makes immersive public sculptures and installations that rethink landscape — using materials like graphite, glazed tile, gold and glass to evoke fire, water, cosmos and the politics of how land is seen.

headshots.credit: Teresita Fernández, photo by Natalia Mantini — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Teresita Fernández: Landscape Is About What You Don't See

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Teresita Fernández was born in 1968 in Miami, Florida, to Cuban-American parents. She studied at Florida International University and Virginia Commonwealth University, and is based in New York. She came to prominence in the late 1990s and 2000s and was awarded a MacArthur Fellowship in 2005. She is also a noted advocate for artists and served on the U.S. Commission of Fine Arts.

Fernández makes sculpture and immersive installations that reimagine landscape — not as something seen from a distance, but as a psychological, material and political experience. Her work draws on geology, natural phenomena, art history and the complex histories of place, colonialism and identity, particularly in the Americas.

biography.heading: Rethinking landscape
biography.text:
Fernández is celebrated for a famous insight that "landscape is more about what you don't see than what you do see." Rather than depicting scenery, she evokes natural phenomena — fire, water, the night sky, minerals, the horizon — through abstract, materially rich works that immerse the viewer and unsettle the conventions of landscape representation.

Her installations use unexpected materials — graphite, glazed ceramic, gold, glass, polished metal, malachite, charcoal — to conjure the experience of being within a landscape or natural event. She is interested in perception, in the way light and material can suggest vast natural forces, and in dismantling the colonial gaze that has historically framed landscape as territory to be possessed.

biography.heading: Materials of the earth
biography.text:
Materials carry meaning in Fernández's work. She has made monumental works from graphite — evoking carbon, geology and drawing itself — and from gold, which in the context of the Americas inevitably summons histories of extraction, colonialism and violence. Her use of charcoal, mineral pigments and stone connects her work to the physical substance of the land and to its layered human and natural histories.

Through these materials she addresses themes of ecological and historical trauma, of what lies buried and unseen beneath the surface of a place. The beauty of her surfaces is always entangled with deeper questions about land, power and memory in the Americas.

biography.heading: Public art and immersive environments
biography.text:
Fernández has created significant public commissions, including the much-admired Fata Morgana, a vast, shimmering golden canopy of perforated metal suspended over a walkway in New York's Madison Square Park, which scattered light and reflection over visitors below. Such works transform public space into an immersive perceptual and contemplative experience.

Her gallery installations similarly envelop the viewer — in fields of glittering material evoking the night sky, in dark graphite landscapes, in surfaces that seem to dissolve the boundary between object and environment. She consistently makes the viewer aware of their own position within, rather than outside, the landscape.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
A MacArthur Fellow with major museum exhibitions and public commissions across the United States, Fernández is recognised as a leading contemporary artist and an important voice on questions of landscape, colonial history and the role of artists in public life.

Her enduring contribution is to have reinvented landscape art for the present — shifting it from passive scenery to an immersive, material and political experience that confronts histories of extraction and the limits of perception. Through earth, gold, graphite and light, Fernández makes landscapes that ask us to reconsider what we see, what we ignore, and what lies beneath.

name: Iván Navarro
artStatement: Navarro builds sculptures from fluorescent and neon light, mirrors and glowing tubes that open illusory infinite tunnels — works charged with memory of dictatorship, power, surveillance and the politics of electricity.

headshots.credit: Iván Navarro, Die Again — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Iván Navarro: This Land Is Your Land

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Iván Navarro was born in 1972 in Santiago, Chile, and grew up under the military dictatorship of Augusto Pinochet — an experience of surveillance, fear, blackouts and state violence that profoundly shaped his art. He later moved to New York, where he has built an international career working with light, especially fluorescent and neon tubes, mirrors and electricity.

Navarro's work is visually dazzling but politically charged. Beneath the seductive glow of his light sculptures lie pointed reflections on power, control, imprisonment, migration and the abuse of authority. He uses the very medium of electric light — which the dictatorship once cut off as a tool of control — as a material for resistance and memory.

biography.heading: Light, power and control
biography.text:
For Navarro, light and electricity are never neutral. Growing up amid the dictatorship's blackouts and its use of electricity in torture, he came to see electric light as bound up with power in every sense — political, electrical and economic. His works harness light's beauty while exposing its darker associations, making the viewer aware of the systems of control that structure modern life.

He frequently works with fluorescent tubes and neon text, materials associated with both commercial spectacle and institutional spaces. The slick, glowing surfaces draw the viewer in, only to deliver unsettling messages about authority, complicity and freedom.

biography.heading: Infinite mirrors and impossible spaces
biography.text:
Navarro is especially known for sculptures that combine light with mirrors and one-way glass to create the illusion of infinite depth. His Water Tower and other works use this technique to make ordinary structures appear to plunge endlessly into the ground or rise without limit — bottomless wells of light, ladders descending into infinity, doorways opening onto impossible voids.

These vertiginous illusions are both wondrous and disquieting. The infinite ladders and tunnels evoke aspiration and entrapment, escape and confinement at once. A glowing ladder that seems to descend forever can read as a route to freedom or a fall into the abyss — an ambiguity that captures the precariousness of those subject to oppressive power.

biography.heading: Migration, words and public space
biography.text:
Navarro's neon and light works often incorporate charged words and phrases — "BED," "LADDER," fragments of political language, the names of borders — turning language into a luminous, ambiguous sign. He has addressed themes of migration and displacement directly, including monumental public installations that speak to the experience of crossing borders and the meaning of land and belonging.

A notable public commission in New York used illuminated water towers emblazoned with the phrase "This Land Is Your Land," at once celebratory and pointedly questioning of who that land truly belongs to. Such works bring his political concerns into shared civic space.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Navarro represented Chile at the Venice Biennale in 2009 and has exhibited internationally at major museums and galleries, with his work held in significant collections. He is recognised as a leading contemporary artist working with light and as a powerful political voice.

His enduring achievement is to have fused the dazzle of light art with serious political reflection, drawing on his own history under dictatorship to create works that are simultaneously beautiful and unnerving. In Navarro's hands, electric light becomes a means of remembering oppression and imagining freedom.

name: Ryoji Ikeda
artStatement: Ikeda composes with raw sound, data and light — sine tones, noise and torrents of numbers rendered as vast monochrome projections and beams — making the scale and abstraction of information into a visceral, immersive experience.

headshots.credit: Ryoji Ikeda, Spectra — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Ryoji Ikeda was born in 1966 in Gifu, Japan. He emerged in the 1990s from the worlds of electronic music and sound art, becoming renowned as a composer of extreme, precise electronic music before extending his practice into immersive audiovisual installation. He is based between Japan and Europe and is widely considered one of the foremost artists working with data, sound and light.

Ikeda's work operates at the intersection of art, music, mathematics and science. He is fascinated by the fundamental nature of data, information, the infinite and the imperceptible — by the vast scales of the universe and the building blocks of digital reality — and he translates these abstract domains into overwhelming sensory experiences.

biography.heading: The aesthetics of data
biography.text:
Ikeda composes with raw data, mathematics and pure electronic signal. His installations typically deploy stark black-and-white visuals — streams of numbers, barcodes, scientific data, flickering patterns and pulsing grids — synchronised with precise, often piercing electronic sound built from sine waves, glitches and frequencies at the edge of human hearing.

The effect is intense and immersive: viewers are enveloped in torrents of information moving faster than the mind can process, confronting the sublime scale and inhuman precision of the data that underpins modern existence. Ikeda transforms the invisible flows of digital information into a visceral, almost physical encounter.

biography.heading: The infinite and the imperceptible
biography.text:
Much of Ikeda's work engages with the extremes of scale and perception — the infinitesimally small and the cosmically vast, frequencies and patterns at the limits of what humans can sense. Projects drawing on particle physics, mathematics, astronomical data and pure number explore the boundary between the perceptible and the imperceptible, the human and the inhuman.

His large-scale works, such as the test pattern series, push image and sound to thresholds of speed and intensity, while data-verse and related projects visualise everything from the human genome to the cosmos. The viewer is confronted with the limits of comprehension — a contemporary, technological version of the mathematical sublime.

biography.heading: Monumental installations
biography.text:
Ikeda is known for spectacular, room-scale and architectural installations. He has projected cascades of data across the floors and walls of vast industrial spaces, created corridors and arrays of light and sound, and staged works in power stations, museums and public sites worldwide. His test pattern works have transformed enormous surfaces into stroboscopic fields of black-and-white information.

These environments are at once rigorous and overwhelming, austere and sublime. They demand bodily presence; the synchronised assault of image and sound cannot be conveyed by reproduction, only experienced in the space. Ikeda's installations turn data into architecture and architecture into an instrument of perception.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Ikeda has exhibited and performed at leading museums, festivals and venues internationally, and is recognised as a pioneering figure in digital and audiovisual art and electronic composition. His work bridges the worlds of contemporary music and visual art.

His enduring contribution is to have forged a rigorous, sublime aesthetic from the raw materials of the digital age — data, signal, mathematics and pure frequency. In an era saturated with information, Ikeda makes that information sensible and overwhelming, confronting audiences with the vast, precise and largely invisible structures that shape the contemporary world.

name: Mariko Mori
artStatement: Mori fuses technology, Buddhism and futurism in photographs, video and luminous sculptures — serene, otherworldly environments that imagine a connection between cosmos, nature, ritual and a hybrid future self.

headshots.credit: Mariko Mori — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Mariko Mori: Between the Ancient Gods and the Future
Rebirth: Recent Work by Mariko Mori

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Mariko Mori was born in 1967 in Tokyo. She studied fashion and worked briefly as a model before turning to art, training in Tokyo and then at art schools in London, including Chelsea College of Art and the Byam Shaw School, and later participating in the Whitney Independent Study Program in New York. She rose to prominence in the 1990s with striking, technologically sophisticated work and has since developed an increasingly spiritual and contemplative practice.

Mori's art fuses advanced technology with ancient spirituality, drawing on Buddhism, Shinto, Japanese history and prehistoric cultures, as well as on science, cosmology and futurism. She envisions a harmonious union of nature, technology and the spiritual, offering a hopeful, transcendent vision rarely found in contemporary art.

biography.heading: Cyborgs and futuristic visions
biography.text:
Mori first became known in the 1990s for elaborately staged photographs and videos in which she appeared as a futuristic cyborg, pop idol or otherworldly deity, set against the backdrop of contemporary Tokyo or imagined sci-fi landscapes. These dazzling, self-created personas explored themes of identity, technology, consumer culture and the place of the spiritual in a hyper-modern society.

The works were visually seductive and technically ambitious, blending fashion, performance and digital effects. Even at this early stage, Mori was interested in transcendence — in the possibility that technology and futurity might open onto spiritual experience rather than simply alienation.

biography.heading: Wave UFO and immersive technology
biography.text:
Mori's ambitions grew toward immersive, participatory environments that fuse cutting-edge technology with meditative experience. Her celebrated work Wave UFO is a sleek, capsule-like pod that visitors enter, where their brainwaves are read by electrodes and translated into real-time visuals projected overhead, creating a shared, biofeedback-driven journey toward a sense of connection and unity.

Such works reflect her vision of technology as a tool for spiritual awakening and interconnection rather than division. They invite participants into states of contemplation and oneness, using the most advanced means to pursue ancient ends.

biography.heading: Nature, ritual and the cosmos
biography.text:
In her later career Mori has turned increasingly toward nature, ritual and cosmology. She founded the Faou Foundation, dedicated to installing permanent artworks in natural sites across the six inhabited continents as a way of honouring nature and fostering harmony. Her installations and sculptures, often luminous, organic and minimal in form, evoke prehistoric standing stones, celestial events and Shinto and Buddhist sacred space.

These works connect deep time — prehistory and the cosmos — with the present, and seek to reawaken a sense of the sacred in the natural world. Light, often responsive to real celestial or natural data, plays a central role, linking earth and sky, the human and the universal.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Mori represented Japan at the Venice Biennale, where she won an honourable mention, and has exhibited at major museums internationally since the 1990s. She is recognised as a distinctive visionary among contemporary artists for her fusion of technology and spirituality.

Her enduring contribution is a singular, optimistic vision that reconciles the technological and the sacred, the futuristic and the ancient. In a field often marked by irony and critique, Mori offers immersive experiences of beauty, transcendence and cosmic connection, proposing that art and technology might help reawaken humanity's bond with nature and the universe.

name: Gerhard Richter
artStatement: One of the most important living painters, Richter moves between photo-based realism and pure abstraction — blurred photo-paintings and squeegee-dragged abstractions that interrogate how images, memory and truth are made and doubted.

headshots.credit: Gerhard Richter, Prague 2017 — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Gerhard Richter Painting: the master at work

biography.heading: Born into a divided Germany
biography.text:
Gerhard Richter was born in 1932 in Dresden, Germany. His childhood and youth were marked by the trauma of the Nazi era and the Second World War — experiences, including the loss of family members on different sides of that history, that would haunt his art for decades. After the war he trained as a mural painter in the socialist realist style of communist East Germany, studying at the Dresden Academy.

In 1961, shortly before the Berlin Wall sealed off the East, Richter fled to West Germany. There he encountered the full range of Western modern and contemporary art and enrolled at the Düsseldorf Art Academy. This passage between two ideological worlds — and two opposed ideas of what art should be — left him permanently sceptical of dogma and ideology, and shaped a career devoted to questioning the very nature of images.

biography.heading: Photo-paintings and the blur
biography.text:
In the early 1960s Richter developed his signature photo-paintings: meticulous paintings based on photographs — from newspapers, family albums and found snapshots — which he then blurred by dragging a brush or other tool across the wet surface. The blur distances the image, denying easy emotion while heightening a sense of memory, loss and the unreliability of representation.

These works range from banal everyday subjects to charged historical and political ones, including his unflinching cycle on the deaths of the Baader-Meinhof terrorists and paintings touching on the Nazi past. By painting from photographs and then obscuring them, Richter probed how images mediate reality and memory, and refused to let painting claim direct access to truth. He has called the photograph "the most perfect picture" precisely because it seems to lack style or opinion.

biography.heading: Abstraction and the squeegee
biography.text:
In parallel with the photo-paintings, Richter pursued abstraction, most famously in his large Abstrakte Bilder. To make these he drags a long squeegee loaded with paint across the canvas, smearing, layering and scraping the surface so that colours mix and reveal one another through chance and erosion as much as design.

The squeegee introduces accident and loss of control into painting, producing richly layered surfaces of extraordinary depth and complexity. Richter has spoken of abstraction as a way of making visible what cannot be seen or named. The coexistence of these abstractions with his hyperrealist photo-paintings is central to his project: he insists on painting's freedom to be many things at once, and refuses any single style or ideology.

biography.heading: Many styles, one inquiry
biography.text:
Richter's restless oeuvre also includes grey monochromes, colour-chart paintings derived from commercial paint samples, glass and mirror works, photographs overpainted with abstraction, and a celebrated stained-glass window for Cologne Cathedral composed of randomly arranged coloured squares. This deliberate stylistic multiplicity is itself a statement — a refusal of the modernist demand for a signature style and a sustained meditation on doubt.

Underlying everything is a profound scepticism toward certainty, whether political, religious or aesthetic. Having lived under both Nazism and communism, Richter distrusts grand narratives and treats painting as a space for questioning rather than asserting. His subject, again and again, is the image itself — its power, its limits and its relationship to reality and memory.

biography.heading: Standing and legacy
biography.text:
Richter is widely regarded as one of the most important painters of the past century, and among the most commercially valuable living artists. His work has been the subject of major retrospectives at museums worldwide, and a deeply personal late cycle, the Birkenau paintings — abstractions based on photographs secretly taken at Auschwitz — confronts the limits of representing atrocity.

His enduring achievement is to have kept painting vital and intellectually serious in an age that repeatedly declared it dead. By embracing photography, abstraction, chance and doubt, Richter created a body of work of immense range and depth that continually interrogates how, and whether, images can hold truth.

name: Tomma Abts
artStatement: Abts paints small, identically-sized abstract canvases built slowly, without reference, layer upon layer — taut geometries, shadows and reversals that seem to generate their own internal logic and light.

headshots.credit: Portrait

works.title: Untitled abstractions
works.medium: Acrylic and oil on canvas (48 × 38 cm)

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Tomma Abts was born in 1967 in Kiel, Germany, and studied in Berlin before moving to London in the mid-1990s, where she has been based ever since. Working quietly and with great discipline, she developed a distinctive and rigorous approach to abstract painting that, in 2006, made her the first painter — and one of relatively few abstract artists — to win the Turner Prize.

Abts works on a deliberately modest and consistent scale: nearly all her paintings are the same small size, 48 by 38 centimetres. Within this self-imposed format she has built an art of remarkable depth and concentration, devoted entirely to abstraction generated from within the act of painting itself.

biography.heading: Painting without a plan
biography.text:
Abts begins each painting without a preconceived image, model or source. She works intuitively, building up forms layer by layer over long periods — sometimes many months — adding, covering and revising until the painting resolves into a finished composition. The geometric and curving forms that emerge are invented through the process rather than planned in advance or drawn from the external world.

This method makes each painting a record of its own making. Earlier states remain faintly visible beneath the surface as ridges and ghostly contours, so that the finished work carries the history of the decisions and reversals that produced it. The paintings are abstract in the purest sense: they refer to nothing outside themselves, originating entirely in the activity of painting.

biography.heading: Depth, light and illusion
biography.text:
Abts's paintings create subtle, often paradoxical sensations of depth, light and three-dimensionality. Through carefully modulated colour, shading and the layering of forms, flat shapes seem to cast shadows, fold, or hover in shallow space, producing quiet optical puzzles. The light in her paintings appears to come from within the forms themselves, with no consistent external source.

The result is an art of concentrated, slow-burning intensity. Her muted, idiosyncratic palette and precise, handmade surfaces reward extended looking, revealing complexities and ambiguities that are invisible at a glance. Each painting is a small, self-contained world governed by its own internal logic.

biography.heading: Discipline and consistency
biography.text:
Abts's career is defined by a rare consistency and restraint. By keeping her format fixed and her process intuitive, she removes the usual variables of scale and subject and focuses entirely on the internal life of the painting. She titles her works with obscure German first names drawn from a dictionary, deliberately avoiding any descriptive meaning and reinforcing the self-sufficiency of each piece.

This monastic discipline runs counter to the spectacle and scale of much contemporary art. Abts makes a powerful case for painting as a slow, private, deeply considered practice, and for abstraction as an inexhaustible field of invention within strict, self-chosen limits.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Since winning the Turner Prize, Abts has been the subject of significant museum exhibitions internationally, including presentations at the Serpentine Galleries in London and at major institutions in Europe and the United States, and her work is held in important collections. She is regarded as one of the most respected abstract painters of her generation.

Her enduring contribution is to have revitalised abstract painting through sheer rigour and concentration — proving that, within a tiny, fixed format and a purely intuitive process, painting can still generate genuine surprise, depth and mystery. Hers is an art of patience, integrity and quiet, hard-won revelation.

name: Mark Bradford
artStatement: Bradford builds large abstract 'paintings' from layered and sanded paper, posters and signage scavenged from the city — maps of social, racial and economic life in which abstraction carries documentary and political weight.

headshots.credit: Mark Bradford — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Mark Bradford was born in 1961 in Los Angeles, where he still lives and works. He grew up in the city and worked for years in his mother's hair salon in South Central LA, coming to art relatively late and earning his degrees from the California Institute of the Arts in his thirties. The experience of his neighbourhood, his identity as a Black gay man, and the textures of urban life are fundamental to his work.

Bradford makes large-scale abstract paintings from unconventional materials, developing what he calls "social abstraction" — abstraction that carries the imprint of real social and political conditions. His work maps the geographies, histories and inequalities of American cities, particularly the marginalised communities he knows intimately.

biography.heading: Painting with paper
biography.text:
Bradford's signature technique uses no conventional paint. Instead, he builds up his surfaces from layers of found paper — billboard posters, merchant signs, end-papers used in hair styling, newsprint and other ephemera scavenged from the urban environment. He glues these in layers, then sands, tears, scrapes and partially excavates the surface, revealing strata of material beneath.

The results are monumental abstract canvases of great physical richness, their cracked and weathered surfaces resembling aerial maps, geological strata or city grids seen from above. The materials themselves carry social meaning — the posters and signs of a particular neighbourhood and economy — so that the abstraction is literally made from the fabric of urban life. Process becomes a metaphor for layering, erasure and the buried histories of place.

biography.heading: Mapping the social landscape
biography.text:
Bradford's paintings frequently evoke maps, networks and the structures of cities, addressing themes of race, class, sexuality, marginalisation and crisis. Works have responded to the AIDS epidemic, to Hurricane Katrina, to redlining and economic injustice, and to the histories of African American communities, while never abandoning the language of abstraction.

This fusion of abstract formal beauty with urgent social content is the heart of his achievement. He insists that abstraction need not be apolitical or detached; in his hands it becomes a way of registering the lived realities of cities and the people too often rendered invisible within them.

biography.heading: Art and social practice
biography.text:
Bradford's commitment to community extends beyond the studio. With others he co-founded Art + Practice, a non-profit in the Leimert Park neighbourhood of Los Angeles that supports foster youth and provides free arts programming, integrating social service with artistic ambition. For Bradford, art and social responsibility are inseparable.

This ethos shaped his major projects, including his representation of the United States at the Venice Biennale in 2017, where he paired a powerful exhibition with a long-term social collaboration in the host city. He consistently links his visibility and resources as a celebrated artist to concrete support for vulnerable communities.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Bradford received a MacArthur Fellowship and represented the United States at the Venice Biennale, and his work is held in and exhibited by major museums worldwide. He is widely regarded as one of the most important American painters of his generation.

His enduring contribution is "social abstraction" — a body of monumental, materially inventive paintings that carry the histories, geographies and injustices of American urban life within their very substance. Bradford has expanded what abstract painting can mean, binding formal power to social conscience and to a deep commitment to his community.

name: Mark Grotjahn
artStatement: Grotjahn is known for his 'Butterfly' perspective paintings and dense, carved impasto 'Face' works — abstractions that play one-point perspective and gestural mark-making against each other with intense colour.

headshots.credit: Mark Grotjahn — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: Face series
works.medium: Oil on cardboard mounted on linen

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Mark Grotjahn was born in 1968 in Pasadena, California, and studied at the University of Colorado, Boulder, and at the University of California, Berkeley. He emerged in the late 1990s in Los Angeles, where he remains based, and has become one of the most prominent American painters of his generation, known for a disciplined yet expressive approach to abstraction.

Grotjahn's work is rooted in a deep engagement with the history of modern art — from Renaissance perspective to abstraction, Cubism and Abstract Expressionism — which he reworks through several long-running, evolving series. His career has been built on the sustained exploration of a few core motifs pushed to ever greater complexity.

biography.heading: The Butterfly paintings
biography.text:
Grotjahn first became widely known for his Butterfly paintings, begun around the turn of the millennium. These works deploy radiating lines that converge toward multiple vanishing points, evoking the wings of a butterfly while playing with the conventions of one-point and multi-point perspective. The result is a tension between flatness and the illusion of deep space, between rigorous geometric structure and luminous colour.

The Butterfly series demonstrates Grotjahn's characteristic method: taking a clear structural device — here, perspectival lines — and exploring its variations exhaustively, in different palettes and configurations. The paintings are at once cool and sensuous, conceptually precise and richly painterly, and they established his reputation.

biography.heading: The Face and Mask works
biography.text:
From the mid-2000s Grotjahn developed his Face paintings, densely worked canvases built from thick, ribboned strokes of oil paint, often suggesting masklike visages with rudimentary eyes and noses amid swirling fields of colour. These works draw on sources ranging from so-called primitivism and tribal masks to Picasso and Abstract Expressionism, and they are far more gestural and impasto-laden than the crisp Butterflies.

He has also made sculptures — bronze masks based on cardboard constructions painted in bright colours — extending the mask motif into three dimensions. Across painting and sculpture, the mask becomes a vehicle for exploring identity, abstraction and art history, while foregrounding the sheer physical pleasure of worked surface and colour.

biography.heading: Process and seriality
biography.text:
Grotjahn's practice is defined by serial investigation: he returns again and again to a limited set of structures — the perspectival butterfly, the mask, geometric grounds — developing them over years into an ever more varied and complex body of work. He is attentive to the history of abstraction and to the dialogue between rigorous structure and expressive mark-making.

This combination of conceptual discipline and painterly intensity, of art-historical awareness and material immediacy, gives his work its distinctive character. He treats painting as both an intellectual problem and a sensuous craft, and refuses to settle into a single mode.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Grotjahn has exhibited extensively with major galleries and museums internationally and is among the most sought-after painters of his generation, his work held in significant collections. His exhibitions, including bodies of work shown at venues such as Gagosian, have been widely reviewed and influential.

His enduring contribution lies in the rigour and richness of his serial approach to abstraction — taking a few elemental structures and mining them for decades to produce work that is at once analytical and lush. Grotjahn has shown that ambitious abstract painting, deeply conversant with its own history, remains a vital and evolving practice.

name: Tomory Dodge
