artStatement: Dodge paints thickly worked canvases that hover between abstraction and landscape — fragmented, gestural fields of strokes and debris that coalesce into image and then dissolve back into paint.

headshots.credit: Portrait

videos.title: Tomory Dodge: An Appetite for Painting

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Tomory Dodge was born in 1974 in Denver, Colorado. He studied at the Rhode Island School of Design and earned his graduate degree at the California Institute of the Arts, settling in Los Angeles, where he has become recognised as a distinctive voice in contemporary abstract painting. He emerged in the 2000s with work that bridges representation and abstraction.

Dodge is known for richly worked, gestural paintings that hover between recognisable imagery and pure abstraction. His work is concerned with the materiality of paint itself, with the act of looking, and with the way the eye assembles coherent images from fragments of mark, colour and texture.

biography.heading: Between image and abstraction
biography.text:
Dodge's earlier paintings often took as their starting points landscapes, wreckage, debris and overlooked scenes — explosions, abandoned places, piles of material — rendered through thick, energetic brushwork. Up close these dissolve into a welter of individual strokes; at a distance they cohere into suggested images. This oscillation between the legible and the abstract is central to his work.

Over time his paintings have moved further toward abstraction, foregrounding the dense accumulation of marks, slabs and ribbons of paint. Yet a sense of place, atmosphere or remembered image often lingers, giving the abstraction an underlying suggestiveness. The viewer is made aware of the process by which perception turns marks into meaning.

biography.heading: The materiality of paint
biography.text:
Dodge's work celebrates the physical substance of oil paint. He applies it thickly and variously — in broad strokes, scraped passages, dabs and ridges — so that the surface becomes a dense field of distinct, tactile marks. The paintings are as much about the behaviour and presence of the medium as about any depicted subject.

This attention to facture connects him to the traditions of gestural and expressionist painting while remaining thoroughly contemporary in sensibility. The fragmentary, almost pixelated quality of his mark-making also resonates with a world of screens and digital images, even as it insists on the handmade and material.

biography.heading: Colour and energy
biography.text:
Dodge's paintings are notable for their vivid, often unexpected colour and their restless energy. Bright passages collide with darker tones; marks pile up and interrupt one another; the eye is kept in constant motion across the surface. There is a sense of controlled chaos, of order emerging from and dissolving back into a flurry of activity.

This dynamism gives his abstraction a charged, alive quality. The paintings reward both the distant view, where images and structures emerge, and the close view, where they break apart into pure painterly incident.

biography.heading: Standing
biography.text:
Dodge has exhibited with galleries in Los Angeles, New York and internationally, and his work is held in public and private collections. He is recognised among the notable abstract painters to emerge from the Los Angeles scene in the twenty-first century.

His enduring contribution is a body of painting that keeps alive the dialogue between abstraction and representation, and that foregrounds the pleasures and possibilities of paint itself. Dodge's canvases dramatise the very act of seeing — the way the eye builds images from fragments — while delighting in colour, gesture and material.

name: Tom McGlynn
artStatement: McGlynn is an American artist, writer and educator whose hard-edged, reductive abstract paintings and works on paper draw on signage and vernacular geometry, pursuing what he calls a 'non-relational' visual order. (He is also an art critic and curator.)

headshots.credit: Portrait

videos.title: In the Studio with Tom McGlynn
Ethan Ryman & Tom McGlynn in Conversation

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Tom McGlynn is an American artist, writer and curator based in the New York area. Trained as a painter, he has developed a body of abstract work grounded in geometry, colour and the legacies of modernism, while also becoming an influential voice in contemporary art discourse through his writing and conversations with other artists.

McGlynn's dual role — as both a maker of austere abstract works and a thoughtful commentator on art — gives his practice an unusually reflective, dialogic character. His painting engages with the history of geometric abstraction and with the visual language of signs, systems and everyday design.

biography.heading: Geometry and the everyday sign
biography.text:
McGlynn's abstract paintings and works on paper employ spare geometric forms, flat planes of colour and carefully balanced compositions. His vocabulary draws on the tradition of hard-edge and geometric abstraction, but it is also informed by the graphic language of signage, branding and the designed environment — the shapes and colour codes that structure everyday visual experience.

This interplay between fine-art abstraction and vernacular design is central to his work. He is interested in how simple geometric forms carry meaning, how colour functions as a kind of signal, and how the legacy of modernist abstraction persists and mutates within the visual culture of daily life.

biography.heading: Restraint and clarity
biography.text:
McGlynn's work is characterised by economy, clarity and restraint. He pares composition down to essential relationships of form and colour, seeking a quiet precision rather than expressive flourish. The paintings invite slow, attentive looking, rewarding the viewer with subtle adjustments of proportion, hue and balance.

This disciplined approach reflects a considered engagement with the questions that have long animated abstract art: the autonomy of form and colour, the relationship between art and design, and the capacity of reduced means to generate meaning and feeling.

biography.heading: Writing and dialogue
biography.text:
Beyond his studio practice, McGlynn is well known as a writer and interviewer, contributing criticism and conducting extended conversations with fellow artists. This work has made him a respected participant in contemporary art discourse, attentive to the ideas and practices of his peers and to the broader conversation around abstraction and painting today.

His critical engagement informs his art, and vice versa: his practice is that of an artist who thinks deeply about the field he works in, and who values exchange, reflection and the community of artists.

biography.heading: Standing
biography.text:
McGlynn has exhibited his work and contributed to contemporary art discourse over a sustained career, and his paintings are held in collections. He occupies a distinctive position as both practitioner and commentator within the world of contemporary abstraction.

His enduring contribution lies in a clear-eyed, reflective approach to geometric abstraction — one that bridges fine art and the designed visual world — and in his role as a thoughtful voice within the ongoing conversation about painting and its possibilities.

name: Sarah Cain
artStatement: Cain makes exuberant, improvisational paintings that spill off the canvas onto floors, walls, windows and found objects — site-responsive installations that treat painting as a physical, immersive and joyful act.

headshots.credit: Sarah Cain, installation at SFO — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: We Will Walk Right Up To the Sun
Now I'm going to tell you everything

videos.title: Art&: A Conversation with Sarah Cain

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Sarah Cain was born in 1979 in Albany, New York. She studied at the San Francisco Art Institute and earned her graduate degree at the University of California, Berkeley, and is based in Los Angeles. She has built a reputation for vibrant, exuberant work that pushes painting off the canvas and into three dimensions and architecture.

Cain's practice expands the language of abstract painting into immersive, site-specific installation. While she makes paintings on canvas, she is best known for transforming entire rooms, floors, walls, staircases and even found objects into sprawling, joyful fields of colour, pattern and gesture.

biography.heading: Painting beyond the canvas
biography.text:
Cain treats space itself as her canvas. In her site-specific installations she paints directly onto floors, walls and architectural surfaces, and incorporates everyday objects — keys, crystals, fabrics, chains, plants, beads — into dense, all-over compositions. The viewer does not stand before her work but walks into and through it, surrounded by colour and incident.

This expansion of painting into the environment is the core of her project. She combines the gestural energy and chromatic intensity of abstract painting with the immersive scale of installation, creating experiences that are at once painterly and spatial, controlled and overflowing with life.

biography.heading: Colour, energy and the handmade
biography.text:
Cain's work is defined by its bold, saturated colour and its sense of spontaneous energy. Her compositions combine geometric patterns, washes, drips, hand-painted motifs and found materials into vivid, maximalist wholes. There is a celebratory, generous quality to the work — a feeling of abundance and freedom.

At the same time, her installations are carefully considered responses to their specific sites, attentive to architecture, light and the path of the viewer. The handmade, personal quality of her mark-making and her incorporation of meaningful objects give the work an intimate, even talismanic dimension amid its exuberance.

biography.heading: Public commissions
biography.text:
Cain has undertaken significant public and institutional commissions, bringing her immersive painting to airports, museums and public buildings, including large-scale works that transform passages and walls into vibrant environments for everyday encounter. The inaugural installation for the Institute of Contemporary Art, Los Angeles, was a notable early statement of her ambition.

These public works extend her practice to broad audiences, turning transitional and institutional spaces into sites of colour, surprise and delight. They demonstrate her commitment to an art that is accessible, generous and woven into lived space.

biography.heading: Standing
biography.text:
Cain has exhibited widely in the United States and internationally and is recognised as a leading figure in the expansion of contemporary abstract painting into installation. Her work is held in public and private collections.

Her enduring contribution is to have liberated abstract painting from the rectangle, carrying its energy, colour and gesture into the full three dimensions of architecture and everyday space. Cain's immersive, joyful environments reimagine what a painting can be and where it can live.

name: Katharina Grosse
artStatement: Grosse uses an industrial spray gun to flood architecture, landscape and objects with sweeping fields of saturated colour — liberating painting from the canvas into immersive, site-swallowing environments.

headshots.credit: Katharina Grosse, Berlin 2011, photo by Oliver Mark — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: The Horse Trotted Another Couple of Metres, Then It Stopped

videos.title: Katharina Grosse: Painting with Color — Art21
Katharina Grosse: On the Edge of Something Else

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Katharina Grosse was born in 1961 in Freiburg, Germany. She studied at the art academies of Münster and Düsseldorf and is based in Berlin. Since the late 1990s she has become internationally renowned for radically expanding the field of painting through her use of the industrial spray gun, with which she applies vivid colour to surfaces of every kind — canvases, objects, architecture and entire landscapes.

Grosse's central proposition is that painting need not be confined to the canvas or the wall. By spraying colour directly onto floors, rooms, buildings, trees, soil and found objects, she liberates painting into space and the world, creating immersive environments of pure, gestural colour.

biography.heading: The spray gun and expanded painting
biography.text:
Grosse's signature tool is the industrial spray gun, which allows her to apply paint without touching the surface, in sweeping arcs and gradients of intense colour. She sprays across whatever is in her chosen site — walls, furniture, piles of earth, rocks, beds, clothing — unifying disparate objects and architecture under fields of luminous, atmospheric colour.

This technique frees the gesture from the limits of the arm and the brush, enabling vast scale and a sense of colour as a kind of weather or light suffusing space. The boundary between painting, sculpture, architecture and landscape dissolves. The viewer is enveloped in colour rather than positioned in front of a discrete picture.

biography.heading: Immersive environments
biography.text:
Grosse is best known for monumental site-specific installations in which she transforms entire buildings, rooms or outdoor sites into immersive painted environments. She has sprayed colour across derelict buildings, museum interiors, beaches and landscapes, and created large fabric and architectural works that envelop the viewer in cascading hues.

These environments are at once exhilarating and disorienting. Colour spills across every surface, defying the usual hierarchies of figure and ground, object and setting. The work insists on the physical, spatial presence of colour and on painting as an experience to be inhabited rather than merely observed.

biography.heading: Colour as autonomous force
biography.text:
For Grosse, colour is an autonomous, almost elemental force — not a description of objects but a phenomenon in its own right, capable of restructuring perception and space. Her practice treats colour as something that can be sprayed across reality itself, blurring distinctions and creating new, vivid orders of experience.

This vision connects her to the history of gestural abstraction while pushing it into wholly new territory. She combines the spontaneity and scale of Abstract Expressionism with the spatial ambitions of installation art, and the precision of the spray gun with the freedom of the sweeping gesture.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Grosse has exhibited at major museums and biennials worldwide and has created landmark public and architectural commissions internationally, including large-scale interventions at prominent institutions and public sites. She is recognised as one of the most significant figures in contemporary painting's expansion into space.

Her enduring contribution is to have redefined painting as an immersive, spatial and environmental practice. Through the spray gun and her fearless engagement with architecture and landscape, Grosse has shown that colour can be a force that transforms the world around us, and that painting can be something we walk into and live inside.

name: Tom Friedman
artStatement: Friedman is a conceptual sculptor who transforms humble materials — paper, foil, sugar cubes, styrofoam, his own materials pushed to obsessive extremes — into witty, precise objects that test perception, scale and the limits of making.

headshots.credit: Tom Friedman — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: In the Studio with Tom Friedman
Public Art Fund Talks: Tom Friedman

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Tom Friedman was born in 1965 in St. Louis, Missouri. He studied graphic illustration as an undergraduate and then earned a graduate degree in sculpture from the University of Illinois at Chicago. He emerged in the early 1990s with a body of conceptually rigorous, often witty sculpture that transforms humble, everyday materials through obsessive labour and ingenuity.

Friedman's art is rooted in process, attention and wonder. He takes ordinary materials — paper, soap, sugar cubes, toothpicks, plastic, aluminium foil, pencil shavings, even nothing at all — and subjects them to painstaking, often absurdly intensive procedures, producing works that astonish through the sheer dedication and inventiveness of their making.

biography.heading: Transforming the everyday
biography.text:
Friedman's signature is the transformation of cheap, familiar stuff into objects of fascination. He has carved a self-portrait into an aspirin, built intricate structures from toothpicks and sugar cubes, made a sphere from his own chewed bubble gum, and constructed elaborate figures from aluminium foil, foam and packaging. Each work foregrounds the time, skill and conceptual play involved in its creation.

These pieces are at once playful and philosophically pointed. They ask what makes something art, where value resides, and how attention and labour can transmute the worthless into the wondrous. Friedman's humour and lightness coexist with a serious inquiry into perception, materials and the nature of the art object.

biography.heading: Concept and absurdity
biography.text:
Friedman works in the lineage of conceptual art, often pushing ideas to deadpan, absurd extremes. He has exhibited a blank pedestal said to be cursed by a witch, a sphere of space said to be a self-portrait, and works defined as much by their improbable premises and instructions as by their physical form. The conceptual framing is integral to the experience.

This blend of conceptual wit and virtuoso making is characteristic. Friedman delights in paradox, illusion and the gap between a work's modest materials and its outsized ambition or premise. The viewer is invited to marvel, to laugh and to think about the conventions of art all at once.

biography.heading: Scale and recent work
biography.text:
While many of his early works are small and intimate, Friedman has increasingly worked at large and even monumental scale, including towering figures fabricated from materials such as stainless steel, roasting pans and aluminium. These public and large-scale sculptures retain his characteristic ingenuity and humour while engaging the body and architecture.

Across scales, his work maintains a consistent spirit of invention, transformation and play. Whether a tiny carved pill or a giant figure made from cookware, each piece embodies his conviction that art can arise from anything, given enough imagination and care.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Friedman has exhibited at major museums and galleries internationally since the 1990s and his work is held in important collections. He is recognised as a distinctive figure in contemporary sculpture, admired for his inventiveness, wit and conceptual rigour.

His enduring contribution is to have demonstrated, again and again, that the most ordinary materials can become extraordinary through attention, labour and imagination. Friedman's work renews a sense of wonder at the made object and keeps alive the conceptual tradition's questioning of what art is and where its value lies.

name: Doris Salcedo
artStatement: Salcedo makes sculpture and public interventions that mourn the victims of political violence — furniture filled with concrete, a fissure cut into a museum floor — turning grief, absence and testimony into materially exacting form.

headshots.credit: Doris Salcedo — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Doris Salcedo was born in 1958 in Bogotá, Colombia, where she continues to live and work. She studied art in Bogotá and then earned a graduate degree in New York before returning to Colombia. Her art is inseparable from the violent history of her country — its decades of civil conflict, political violence, disappearances and displacement — and from a broader concern with mourning, memory and the victims of atrocity worldwide.

Salcedo makes sculpture and installations of great quietness and moral gravity. Rather than depicting violence, she gives form to absence, loss and grief, creating works that act as memorials to the unnamed and the disappeared. Extensive interviews with victims and survivors underpin her practice.

biography.heading: Furniture, absence and memory
biography.text:
Salcedo is known for sculptures made from everyday domestic furniture — chairs, tables, wardrobes, beds — often belonging to or evoking victims of violence. She alters these objects in haunting ways: filling wardrobes and cabinets with concrete, embedding garments or fragments within them, fusing pieces of furniture together. The result is a sense of suffocation, stillness and irrecoverable loss.

These works speak of homes destroyed and lives extinguished, of the domestic intimacy shattered by political violence. The furniture, made useless and silent, becomes a vessel for memory and mourning. Salcedo's restraint — her refusal of spectacle or explicit imagery — gives the works their profound, almost unbearable power.

biography.heading: Monuments to the disappeared
biography.text:
Salcedo has created extraordinary public works and ephemeral actions commemorating specific atrocities. She has lowered chairs down the façade of a building to mark a massacre, filled a public square with thousands of chairs evoking the disappeared, and covered a plaza in roses in memory of murdered victims. These temporary monuments transform civic space into sites of collective mourning.

Her most famous public work is Shibboleth, created for the Turbine Hall at Tate Modern in 2007: a long, jagged crack running the length of the hall's floor. The fissure evoked division, exclusion and the experience of immigrants and the marginalised — a literal and metaphorical rupture in the foundation of a great institution. Even after being filled, it left a permanent scar.

biography.heading: Materials, time and labour
biography.text:
Salcedo's works are made with immense, painstaking labour, often over long periods and with teams of assistants and specialists. She has sewn rose petals together, grown grass through suspended chairs, and developed complex techniques to embed and transform her materials. This slow, careful labour is itself a form of mourning and respect.

Time is central to her work — the time of grief, of memory, of the slow processes of making and decay. Her installations frequently involve fragile, perishable or laboriously worked materials, embodying the precariousness of life and the persistence of loss. Beauty and horror, tenderness and violence are held in tense balance.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Salcedo has exhibited at major museums and biennials worldwide, received significant international awards, and been the subject of major retrospectives. She is regarded as one of the most powerful and morally serious artists of her generation.

Her enduring contribution is to have created a profound art of mourning — one that gives dignified, eloquent form to the absent and the disappeared without resorting to spectacle. Through silent furniture, cracked floors and fields of roses, Salcedo bears witness to political violence and insists on the value of remembering its victims.

name: Haroon Mirza
artStatement: Mirza orchestrates electricity, LEDs, sound and found media into sculptural installations where current itself is the medium — composing rhythmic circuits that blur the line between noise, music, light and sculpture.

headshots.credit: Portrait

works.title: The National Apavilion of Then and Now

videos.title: Lisson Gallery talks to Haroon Mirza

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Haroon Mirza was born in 1977 in London, where he is based. He studied painting, design and then fine art, completing a graduate degree at the Slade School of Fine Art. He emerged around 2010 as a leading artist working with sound, light and electricity, winning the Silver Lion at the Venice Biennale in 2011 for a younger artist.

Mirza creates installations and sculptural assemblages in which electricity is the central, unifying medium. He orchestrates light, sound and electrical current into rhythmic, often hypnotic compositions, exploring the relationships between noise and music, perception and energy, and the visible and invisible forces that power modern life.

biography.heading: Electricity as medium
biography.text:
At the core of Mirza's practice is the idea of electricity itself as the primary material. He builds circuits that drive LEDs, speakers and household and salvaged objects, synchronising flickering light with pulsing sound so that the viewer experiences the flow of current directly as rhythm and pattern. Wires, components and devices are left visible, exposing the apparatus.

His works often turn electrical signal into both image and sound simultaneously, revealing that light and noise are, at root, the same energy manifesting differently. This insight gives his installations their distinctive character: they are at once sculptures, light works and musical compositions generated by circuitry.

biography.heading: Noise, music and perception
biography.text:
Mirza is deeply interested in the boundary between noise and music, and between random and ordered sound. Drawing on his interest in music cultures and in the history of experimental sound art, he composes with hums, buzzes, clicks and tones produced by his electrical systems, organising apparent noise into compelling rhythmic structures.

This concern connects to broader questions of perception and meaning: how the mind organises sensory chaos into pattern, how we distinguish signal from noise, and how light and sound shape experience. His installations create immersive, trance-like environments that heighten the viewer's awareness of these processes.

biography.heading: Assemblage and energy systems
biography.text:
Mirza's installations often take the form of assemblages combining found furniture, electronic equipment, salvaged objects, LEDs and speakers into composed "stations" or environments. More recently he has engaged with solar power and the politics and poetics of energy, creating works and sites that harness and dramatise renewable electricity.

This expansion toward energy systems and the natural world gives his work an increasingly ecological and even cosmic dimension, linking the circuitry of his installations to the larger flows of power that sustain society and the planet.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Since his Venice Biennale prize, Mirza has exhibited at major museums and galleries internationally and his work is held in significant collections. He is recognised as a leading contemporary artist working at the intersection of sound, light and visual art.

His enduring contribution is to have made electricity itself into an artistic medium — orchestrating current, light and sound into immersive compositions that reveal the hidden energies and rhythms of the modern world. Mirza's work expands the languages of both sculpture and music, and attunes the viewer to the electrical pulse underlying contemporary life.

name: Rafael Lozano-Hemmer
artStatement: Lozano-Hemmer makes large-scale interactive installations using robotics, sensors, light and biometric data — public works completed only by participation, recording heartbeats, breath and presence as collective portraits.

headshots.credit: Rafael Lozano-Hemmer — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Rafael Lozano-Hemmer was born in 1967 in Mexico City. He studied physical chemistry in Canada before turning to art, and that scientific training underlies his pioneering work in interactive and technological media. He is based between Montreal and Mexico City and is recognised as one of the foremost artists working with technology, interactivity and large-scale public participation.

Lozano-Hemmer creates interactive installations that use robotics, computerised surveillance, sensors, projection, sound and biometric technologies to involve the public directly in the artwork. His central concern is the relationship between technology, the body, the public and the urban environment — turning the tools of surveillance and control into instruments of connection and participation.

biography.heading: Relational architecture
biography.text:
Lozano-Hemmer first became internationally known for his "relational architecture" — large-scale public interventions that transform buildings and public squares through technology and crowd participation. In landmark works, he installed banks of robotic searchlights that members of the public could control via the internet, sculpting vast architectures of light over cities such as Mexico City and along international borders.

These works reclaim public space and monumental technology for collective, playful, democratic ends. By letting ordinary people steer the lights or shape the installation, he turns spectators into co-authors and transforms the alienating apparatus of urban control into a shared civic experience.

biography.heading: Biometrics and the trace of the body
biography.text:
A major strand of Lozano-Hemmer's work captures and records the biometric traces of participants — heartbeats, voices, breath, fingerprints, portraits. His celebrated Pulse series records visitors' heartbeats and translates them into pulsing lights or sound, building vast archives of the living rhythms of everyone who has taken part. Pulse Room fills a space with bulbs flashing to the heartbeats of past participants; Pulse Topology builds landscapes of glowing pulses.

These works are both technologically sophisticated and deeply human, even poignant. By recording the most intimate signs of life and presence, they create collective portraits of communities and meditate on memory, mortality and the trace each person leaves. The cold instruments of biometric measurement become tender memorials to shared existence.

biography.heading: Surveillance, participation and control
biography.text:
Lozano-Hemmer's work consistently engages with surveillance technologies — cameras, tracking, recognition systems — but redirects them toward participation, connection and critical reflection. His installations make visible the technologies that increasingly monitor public life, while inviting people to play with and reclaim them.

This dual stance — fascination with technology's capacities and critical awareness of its dangers — gives his work both its appeal and its substance. He turns the gallery and the public square into spaces where people can experience, question and momentarily control the systems that pervade contemporary society.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Lozano-Hemmer represented Mexico at the Venice Biennale and has exhibited and installed major works at museums, public sites and festivals worldwide, including significant survey exhibitions. He is regarded as a leading figure in new-media and interactive art.

His enduring contribution is to have placed public participation at the centre of technological art — transforming surveillance, robotics and biometrics into instruments of connection, reflection and collective experience. Lozano-Hemmer's work shows how the technologies that monitor and divide us might also be turned toward wonder, intimacy and community.

name: Nairy Baghramian
artStatement: Baghramian makes elegant, bodily abstract sculptures in cast metal, glass, silicone and wax that lean, prop and rest against architecture — probing the body, support, fragility and the relationship of object to space.

headshots.credit: Nairy Baghramian, Beliebte Stellen — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Nairy Baghramian on Listening to Sculpture

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Nairy Baghramian was born in 1971 in Isfahan, Iran, and moved to Germany as a teenager, settling in Berlin, where she is based. She came to prominence in the 2000s and has become one of the most respected sculptors of her generation, known for elegant, enigmatic works that explore the body, support, fragility and the conventions of sculpture and display.

Baghramian's sculpture is abstract but bodily, often evoking limbs, organs, prosthetics or furniture without depicting them directly. She works with materials such as cast metal, polished and pigmented surfaces, glass, silicone, wax and lacquered forms, frequently combining hard and soft, rigid and yielding elements in poised, precarious relationships.

biography.heading: Bodies, supports and fragility
biography.text:
A central theme in Baghramian's work is the relationship between objects and their supports — the often-overlooked structures, brackets, props and armatures that hold things up. She makes the support as important as the supported, drawing attention to dependency, vulnerability and the hidden labour of holding together. Her forms frequently lean, rest against walls, or rely on visible props.

Her sculptures often suggest bodies and their fragilities — joints, limbs, prosthetic or anatomical forms — in abstract, elegant guises. This bodily resonance, combined with her attention to support and balance, gives the work a quiet emotional and even political charge, evoking themes of care, frailty and interdependence.

biography.heading: Material and surface
biography.text:
Baghramian is highly attentive to material and surface. She juxtaposes industrial and handmade qualities, glossy and matte finishes, hard metal and soft, fleshy silicone or wax. Her refined, often unexpected palette and her precise, sensuous surfaces give the works a distinctive presence — at once cool and intimate, abstract and corporeal.

This material sophistication serves her conceptual concerns. The contrast between the polished and the vulnerable, the structural and the soft, embodies the tensions she explores between strength and fragility, autonomy and dependence, the object and its means of display.

biography.heading: Display and the conventions of sculpture
biography.text:
Baghramian frequently reflects on the conditions under which sculpture is shown — the pedestal, the bracket, the gallery architecture, the act of installation. By incorporating and exposing these usually invisible supports, she questions the conventions of presentation and the relationships of power and dependency they embody.

Her major commissions, including a celebrated installation on the façade of the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York, extend these concerns to architecture and the public realm, engaging with how artworks are positioned, supported and seen. The work consistently turns attention to what usually goes unnoticed.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Baghramian has exhibited at major museums and biennials worldwide, including documenta and the Venice Biennale, received significant awards such as the Nasher Prize, and been the subject of important solo exhibitions. She is regarded as a leading contemporary sculptor.

Her enduring contribution is a subtle, intelligent reinvention of sculpture that foregrounds support, fragility and the body, and that questions the conventions of display. Baghramian's poised, enigmatic works invite slow contemplation and reveal the dependencies and vulnerabilities that underlie both objects and lives.

name: Carsten Höller
artStatement: Trained as a scientist, Höller designs experiential installations — giant slides, rotating beds, mirror carousels and perception-altering devices — that treat the visitor as a test subject and doubt as a creative method.

headshots.credit: Carsten Höller, slide at LUMA Arles — Image via Wikimedia Commons

videos.title: Carsten Höller: Test Site, Tate Modern (2006)
Carsten Höller: The Luxury of Doubt

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Carsten Höller was born in 1961 in Brussels to German parents. Unusually among artists, he earned a doctorate in agricultural science, specialising in insect communication, before turning to art in the early 1990s. He is based in Sweden and Belgium. His scientific background profoundly shapes his practice, which treats art as a form of experiment and the viewer as a participant in investigations of perception, behaviour and emotion.

Höller's work uses the methods and apparatus of science — experiments, devices, controlled conditions — to explore human experience, doubt and the nature of happiness. He is interested in how perception can be altered, how certainty can be undermined, and how art can produce genuine changes in the bodies and minds of those who engage with it.

biography.heading: The slides
biography.text:
Höller is most famous for his giant slides, which he has installed in museums and public buildings as functional artworks. Visitors descend through spiralling metal tubes, experiencing a rush of speed, disorientation and what he describes as a particular emotional state — a mix of delight and "voluptuous panic." The slides are at once sculptures, architecture and machines for producing a specific human experience.

His celebrated installation Test Site filled the Turbine Hall at Tate Modern with enormous slides, and he has installed slides in many other institutions and cities. For Höller, the slide is both a practical means of transport and an instrument for transforming the rider's emotional and perceptual state — art that acts directly on the body.

biography.heading: Doubt, perception and experiment
biography.text:
Höller's broader practice is devoted to inducing doubt and altering perception. He has created upside-down goggles that invert the visual field, rotating beds and rooms, mirror works, and "Flying Machines" that lift visitors into the air. His Pill Clock dispenses pills at regular intervals; his sensory deprivation and flotation works alter consciousness. Many pieces require active participation and produce real physiological and psychological effects.

These works treat the viewer as an experimental subject and art as a means of questioning the reliability of perception and the certainties of everyday experience. Höller is fascinated by ambivalence and the suspension of judgement, and his installations are designed to destabilise habitual ways of sensing and knowing.

biography.heading: Happiness, love and the senses
biography.text:
Höller has increasingly explored themes of happiness, love, desire and the senses, creating environments — including a hotel room within a museum, dining experiences and works engaging taste and smell — that turn the investigation of pleasure and emotion into participatory art. He treats subjective experience itself as a legitimate field of experiment.

This pursuit gives his work a playful, hedonistic surface underlain by serious inquiry. Whether through a slide, a flying machine or a transformed bedroom, Höller asks what produces happiness and altered states, and invites visitors to become willing participants in his open-ended experiments.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Höller has exhibited at major museums and biennials worldwide, with landmark installations at institutions including Tate Modern, and his work is widely collected and discussed. He is recognised as a leading figure in participatory and experiential contemporary art.

His enduring contribution is to have fused the methods of science with the experience of art, turning museums into laboratories of perception, doubt and pleasure. Höller's slides, machines and experiments invite the public to question their senses and certainties — and to discover that art can change, however briefly, how it feels to be alive.

name: Monika Sosnowska
artStatement: Sosnowska makes large steel sculptures that twist, crush and contort the modernist architecture of post-war Poland — staircases, gates and facades wrenched into knots that register utopian ambition and its collapse.

headshots.credit: Monika Sosnowska, Stairs — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Monika Sosnowska was born in 1972 in Ryki, Poland. She studied painting in Poznań and then at the Rijksakademie in Amsterdam, before turning decisively to sculpture and installation. Based in Warsaw, she has become one of the most significant sculptors to emerge from Central Europe, known for monumental works that grapple with architecture, history and the legacy of modernism.

Sosnowska's work draws on the built environment of post-war Poland — the utopian modernist and socialist architecture of her childhood, much of it now decaying or demolished. She transforms the structures and materials of this architecture into twisted, distorted sculptures that evoke histories of idealism, failure and change.

biography.heading: Architecture twisted and broken
biography.text:
Sosnowska's signature works take recognisable architectural elements — staircases, gates, railings, reinforced-concrete columns, window grilles, facades — and crumple, bend, fold or collapse them as if subjected to enormous force. Fabricated from steel and concrete with industrial precision, these massive forms appear violently deformed, like the wreckage of buildings or the contortion of utopian structures under the pressure of history.

The effect is both physically imposing and conceptually charged. The mangled staircase that leads nowhere, the twisted gate, the buckled column — all evoke the failure of modernist and socialist architectural dreams, the instability of structures meant to endure, and the violence of historical change. The works hover between abstraction and ruined architecture.

biography.heading: Space, disorientation and modernism
biography.text:
Sosnowska is deeply concerned with the psychology of space and architecture. Earlier in her career she created disorienting room-scale installations and labyrinthine environments that played with scale, perspective and the viewer's spatial bearings, producing uncanny, dreamlike or claustrophobic experiences.

Her engagement with modernism is critical and historically specific. The architecture she references carried social and political ideals — of progress, equality, rational order — that were never fully realised and have since fallen into disrepair or been swept away. By distorting these forms, she reflects on the gap between utopian aspiration and lived reality, and on the way the built environment embodies ideology and memory.

biography.heading: Material and fabrication
biography.text:
Sosnowska's mature works are made with the materials and methods of construction itself — steel, rebar, concrete, paint — fabricated at architectural scale and weight. This material authenticity is essential: the works are not models or representations but real, massive structures, deformed through genuine industrial processes.

The contrast between the rigidity of the materials and the apparent fluidity of their distortion gives the sculptures their power. Heavy steel seems to fold like ribbon; concrete columns appear to wilt. This impossible-seeming deformation of obdurate materials embodies the forces of history acting on the structures and ideals of the past.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Sosnowska represented Poland at the Venice Biennale in 2007 with a major installation, and has exhibited at leading museums and biennials internationally, including documenta. Her work is held in significant collections.

Her enduring contribution is a powerful sculptural language drawn from architecture and history — monumental works that twist the forms of post-war modernism into eloquent emblems of utopian aspiration, failure and transformation. Sosnowska gives physical, visceral form to the weight of history pressing on the structures we build and the ideals they embody.

name: Josiah McElheny
artStatement: McElheny is a sculptor working primarily in hand-blown glass and mirror, building dazzling reflective assemblages and cosmological models that fuse modernist design, history and ideas about infinity and light.

headshots.credit: Portrait

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Josiah McElheny was born in 1966 in Boston. He trained intensively as a glassblower, apprenticing with master craftspeople in the United States and Europe, before bringing this rare technical mastery into the world of contemporary conceptual art. He is based in New York and is widely regarded as one of the most intellectually ambitious artists working with glass and mirror.

McElheny's work fuses extraordinary craftsmanship with deep engagement in the history of ideas — the histories of modernism, design, cosmology, science and utopian thought. He uses glass, mirror and meticulously crafted objects, often combined with text, display cases and museological presentation, to explore memory, reflection, infinity and the dream of perfection.

biography.heading: Glass, mirror and the modernist dream
biography.text:
McElheny is celebrated for highly refined hand-blown glass objects, frequently mirrored or chrome-finished, and for installations that reference the history of modern design and architecture. He has recreated and reimagined modernist glassware, fictional dialogues between designers, and the utopian aspirations embedded in twentieth-century ideas of transparency, reflection and perfection.

Mirrored and reflective surfaces are central to his work. By making objects that reflect endlessly into one another, or that turn clear glass into impenetrable mirror, he creates dazzling, infinite regressions that evoke both the seductions and the illusions of modernist utopias. The works are exquisite, but they carry a critical reflection on the dreams they embody.

biography.heading: Cosmology and infinity
biography.text:
A major strand of McElheny's work engages with cosmology and the structure of the universe. In collaboration with scientists, he has created sculptures modelling the Big Bang and the expanding cosmos as glittering, chandelier-like structures of glass, metal and light — translating astrophysical models into dazzling, suspended forms.

These works connect his fascination with reflection and infinity to the largest possible subject: the origin and shape of the universe. They exemplify his method of grounding visually spectacular objects in rigorous intellectual and scientific content, so that beauty and knowledge are inseparable.

biography.heading: History, display and narrative
biography.text:
McElheny frequently frames his objects within elaborate narratives, historical references and museological displays — vitrines, mirrored cabinets, staged settings — that turn each work into a meditation on how knowledge, memory and value are constructed and presented. He draws on the histories of craft, design, science and modern art, often weaving real and invented stories together.

This conceptual framing distinguishes his work from craft for its own sake. The dazzling glass and mirror objects are vehicles for ideas about modernity, utopia, time and the cosmos, and about the very conventions of museum display through which we encounter art and history.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
McElheny received a MacArthur Fellowship and has exhibited at major museums internationally, with his work held in leading collections. He is recognised as a singular figure who has brought the craft of glass into the heart of conceptual contemporary art.

His enduring contribution is to have united virtuoso glassmaking with profound intellectual inquiry — creating works of breathtaking beauty that reflect, quite literally, on modernism, cosmology, infinity and the construction of knowledge. McElheny shows that craft and concept, beauty and ideas, can be fused into a single luminous practice.

name: Tony Oursler
artStatement: A pioneer of video sculpture, Oursler projects talking faces and eyes onto dolls, eggs and sculptural forms — uncanny, muttering effigies that explore media, identity, psychology and our entanglement with screens.

headshots.credit: Tony Oursler — Image via Wikimedia Commons

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Tony Oursler was born in 1957 in New York, into a family with deep connections to media and entertainment. He studied at the California Institute of the Arts in the late 1970s, emerging from a vibrant scene of video and performance art. He is based in New York and is recognised as a pioneer of video art and a key figure in the artistic exploration of technology, media and the psyche.

Oursler's work probes the pervasive influence of media, screens and technology on human consciousness, identity and emotion. From early single-channel videos to his celebrated video sculptures and large-scale installations, he has consistently explored how the moving image shapes — and distorts — our inner lives.

biography.heading: Talking heads and video sculpture
biography.text:
Oursler is best known for his "video sculptures," in which he projects moving images of faces — often distorted, anguished, muttering or pleading — onto three-dimensional objects: lumpy effigies, dolls, eggs, spheres and abstract forms. The result is uncanny: disembodied faces and eyes come alive on strange surfaces, speaking, watching and emoting, hovering between the human and the artificial.

These works give voice to a kind of media-saturated unconscious — fragmented personalities, anxieties, obsessions and the chatter of a culture mediated by screens. They are eerie, funny and disturbing, animating inanimate objects with the projected presence of human (or quasi-human) consciousness, and implicating the viewer in their gaze and address.

biography.heading: Media, the mind and the uncanny
biography.text:
Oursler's central subject is the relationship between technology, media and the human psyche. He explores how television, film, the internet and surveillance shape perception, belief and identity, and he draws on the histories of optical illusion, spiritualism, hypnosis and early media technologies. His work treats the screen as a site where reality, fantasy and the unconscious blur.

This interest gives his art both a playful, theatrical quality and a serious critical edge. By making screens and projections behave in unexpected, embodied and unsettling ways, he reveals the strangeness of our everyday immersion in mediated images and the psychological effects of living among screens.

biography.heading: Scale, immersion and history
biography.text:
Oursler has worked across an enormous range, from intimate video sculptures to immense outdoor projections and immersive installations filling entire rooms with faces, eyes, voices and imagery. He has also created performances and works tracing the history of optical and media technologies — magic lanterns, phantasmagoria, early cinema, facial-recognition and the smartphone — situating contemporary media within a long lineage of illusion and spectacle.

These large-scale and historically informed works extend his inquiry into the cultural and psychological power of the image. Whether intimate or monumental, his installations envelop the viewer in a world where technology speaks, watches and feels, dramatising our entanglement with media.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Oursler has exhibited at major museums and biennials worldwide since the 1980s, and his work is held in leading collections. He is recognised as a foundational figure in video and media art and an influential explorer of technology's effect on the human mind.

His enduring contribution is to have given vivid, uncanny form to our relationship with screens and media — projecting consciousness onto objects and revealing the anxieties, illusions and obsessions of a culture saturated by images. Oursler's talking effigies remain among the most memorable embodiments of the psychological life of the media age.

name: Refik Anadol
artStatement: Anadol is a pioneer of data-driven art, using machine-learning models trained on vast datasets to generate immersive 'data sculptures' and 'machine hallucinations' — fluid, dreaming projections that visualize memory, nature and the mind of the machine.

headshots.credit: Refik Anadol — Image via Wikimedia Commons

works.title: Unsupervised
works.status: Museum of Modern Art, New York

videos.title: Refik Anadol: AI with a Thinking Brush

biography.heading: Background
biography.text:
Refik Anadol was born in 1985 in Istanbul, Turkey. He studied at Istanbul Bilgi University and then earned graduate degrees at the University of California, Los Angeles, where he became immersed in media arts and emerging technologies. Based in Los Angeles, he leads a studio of designers, architects, data scientists and researchers, and has become one of the most prominent artists working with data and artificial intelligence.

Anadol pioneers the use of machine learning and vast datasets as artistic media, creating immersive, often monumental installations he describes as "data sculptures" and "machine hallucinations." His work explores the aesthetics of data, memory and machine intelligence, and the possibilities of art at the meeting point of the human mind and the computer.

biography.heading: Data as pigment
biography.text:
Anadol treats data as a raw material — a kind of pigment or building block — to be sculpted into flowing, dynamic imagery. Using machine-learning algorithms trained on enormous collections of images, sounds or other information, he generates ever-changing visual fields that surge, swirl and morph across large screens, projections and architectural surfaces.

The results are mesmerising, fluid and immersive: oceans of colour and form that appear to think, dream and remember in real time. By visualising the processes of AI and the patterns latent within massive datasets, Anadol makes the abstract operations of machine intelligence sensible and spectacular, turning information into a living, aesthetic experience.

biography.heading: Machine Hallucinations and Unsupervised
biography.text:
Anadol's ongoing Machine Hallucinations series uses AI to process millions of images — of cities, nature, space, or museum collections — generating dreamlike, constantly evolving visuals that he likens to the machine's own imagination or memory. These works have been projected across building facades, immersive rooms and giant screens around the world.

His celebrated work Unsupervised, created for the Museum of Modern Art in New York, fed the museum's vast collection into an AI model that continuously reinterpreted and reimagined the history of modern art as a churning, ever-changing data sculpture. Such works raise provocative questions about creativity, memory and the relationship between human and machine imagination.

biography.heading: Architecture, nature and immersion
biography.text:
Anadol frequently works at architectural and environmental scale, projecting his data sculptures onto the surfaces of famous buildings, filling immersive rooms, and creating large-scale public spectacles. He has also turned to nature, training models on data about the natural world — coral, rainforests, weather — to create works reflecting on ecology and the planet.

These immersive environments envelop audiences in flowing, responsive imagery, often accompanied by generative sound. They reflect his belief that data and AI can produce new forms of beauty and new ways of experiencing memory, nature and the collective imagination, accessible to broad public audiences.

biography.heading: Recognition
biography.text:
Anadol has exhibited and installed major works at museums, public sites and events worldwide, including MoMA and prominent international venues, and has become one of the most visible artists associated with AI and data art. His work has reached vast audiences and sparked wide debate.

His enduring contribution — still unfolding — is to have brought the aesthetics of data and machine learning into the mainstream of contemporary art, creating immersive works that visualise the imagination of machines. Anadol stands at the forefront of art's encounter with artificial intelligence, exploring what it means to create, remember and dream in collaboration with the computer.
